background_cases: 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal' — the conclusion is entailed.
'If it rains the ground is wet; it rains; therefore the ground is wet' — the conclusion follows necessarily.
'No birds are mammals; all sparrows are birds; therefore no sparrows are mammals' — again, entailment holds.
definition: A conclusion is entailed by a set of premises if and only if (i) denying the conclusion while affirming the premises leads to a contradiction, (ii) the inference can be formalised in a logical system, and (iii) the conclusion adds no information not already contained in the premises.
conditions: denial leads to contradiction
inference formalisable
conclusion adds no new information
counterexample: From 'All dogs bark' and 'Rex is a dog,' it is entailed that 'Rex barks.' But the conclusion, though entailed, does seem to add information in a psychological or practical sense: one may not have considered Rex specifically until the inference was drawn. The third condition conflates logical containment with informational novelty.
abductive_insight: The background cases all had conclusions that seemed intuitively 'contained' in the premises. The definition mistook this appearance of containment for a general feature of entailment, but deductive conclusions can be psychologically novel even when logically implicit.
passage:
Consider three cases of entailment. 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal' — the conclusion is plainly entailed. 'If it rains the ground is wet; it rains; therefore the ground is wet' — the conclusion follows necessarily. 'No birds are mammals; all sparrows are birds; therefore no sparrows are mammals' — again, the entailment is beyond dispute. From these cases one might extract a definition: a conclusion is entailed by a set of premises if and only if denying the conclusion while affirming the premises leads to a contradiction, the inference can be formalised in a logical system, and the conclusion adds no information not already contained in the premises.

But the third condition proves troublesome. Consider: 'All dogs bark; Rex is a dog; therefore Rex barks.' The conclusion is certainly entailed — denying it while affirming the premises leads to a contradiction, and the inference is formally valid. But does the conclusion add no information? In a logical sense, perhaps not — the conclusion is implicit in the premises. But in an intellectual or practical sense, the conclusion may be genuinely informative: one may not have considered Rex in particular before drawing the inference. The background cases were all simple enough that the conclusion seemed obviously contained in the premises. The definition mistook this appearance for a necessary feature of entailment, confusing logical containment with absence of intellectual novelty.

background_cases: A square is defined as a rectangle all of whose sides are equal; this definition picks out precisely the squares.
A prime is defined as an integer greater than one divisible only by one and itself; this picks out the primes.
A bachelor is defined as an unmarried adult male; this picks out the bachelors.
definition: A concept is definable if and only if (i) necessary and sufficient conditions for its application can be stated, (ii) the conditions are finitely expressible, and (iii) competent speakers agree on the extension of the concept.
conditions: necessary and sufficient conditions statable
finitely expressible
competent speakers agree on extension
counterexample: The concept of a game: competent speakers agree on clear cases (chess is a game, taxation is not), the concept is finitely expressible in natural language, yet no set of necessary and sufficient conditions captures all and only games. The first condition fails, yet the concept is perfectly usable.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved tidy, well-bounded concepts (square, prime, bachelor). The definition assumed all concepts have this crisp structure, but many important concepts are held together by family resemblance rather than by necessary and sufficient conditions.
passage:
Consider three cases of successful definition. A square is defined as a rectangle all of whose sides are equal; this definition picks out precisely the squares and nothing else. A prime is defined as an integer greater than one divisible only by one and itself; this picks out precisely the primes. A bachelor is defined as an unmarried adult male; this picks out the bachelors. From these cases one might conclude that a concept is definable if and only if necessary and sufficient conditions for its application can be stated, the conditions are finitely expressible, and competent speakers agree on the extension of the concept.

But consider the concept of a game. Competent speakers of English agree on the clear cases: chess is a game, football is a game, ring-a-ring-o'-roses is a game, taxation is not a game, ploughing a field is not a game. Agreement on the extension exists, at least for central cases. And the concept is finitely expressible in natural language. But no one has succeeded in stating necessary and sufficient conditions that capture all and only games. Some games involve competition, others do not. Some have rules, others are improvisatory. Some are played for amusement, others professionally. The first condition fails, yet the concept is perfectly serviceable. The background cases all involved tidy, well-bounded concepts; the definition assumed all concepts share this structure, but many of our most important concepts are united by family resemblance rather than by a common set of necessary and sufficient conditions.

background_cases: The proposition 'P or not-P' is true under every assignment of truth-values; we call it a tautology.
The proposition 'If P, then P' is true under every assignment; it too is a tautology.
The proposition 'Not (P and not-P)' is true under every assignment; it is likewise a tautology.
definition: A proposition is a tautology if and only if (i) it is a well-formed formula of propositional logic, (ii) its truth can be verified by the truth-table method, and (iii) it is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its atomic components.
conditions: well-formed formula of propositional logic
verifiable by truth-table method
true under every assignment
counterexample: The proposition 'For all x, if x is a bachelor then x is unmarried' is true under every interpretation of the predicate symbols and cannot be falsified. Yet it is not a well-formed formula of propositional logic — it belongs to predicate logic — and its truth cannot be verified by any finite truth-table. It is a logical truth, but not a tautology in the narrow sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all propositional formulae with finitely many atomic components. The definition captured this finiteness but excluded logical truths whose necessity arises from the structure of quantifiers and predicates rather than from truth-functional composition.
passage:
Consider three familiar tautologies. The proposition 'P or not-P' is true under every assignment of truth-values to its atomic components; we recognise it as the law of excluded middle. The proposition 'If P, then P' is similarly immune to falsification by any assignment. And the proposition 'Not both P and not-P' is a tautology expressing the principle of non-contradiction. From these cases one might define: a proposition is a tautology if and only if it is a well-formed formula of propositional logic, its truth can be verified by the truth-table method, and it is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its atomic components.

But this definition proves too narrow when one moves beyond the propositional calculus. Consider the proposition 'For all x, if x is a bachelor then x is unmarried.' This proposition cannot be falsified under any interpretation of the predicate symbols; it is, in an important sense, necessarily true. Yet it is not a well-formed formula of propositional logic. It contains quantifiers and predicate symbols that have no place in the propositional calculus. Nor can its truth be verified by the truth-table method, for the truth-table method applies only to formulae composed of a finite number of propositional variables joined by truth-functional connectives. The background cases were all propositional in character, and the definition was tailored to them. It captured what makes certain propositional formulae necessarily true but excluded an entire class of logical truths whose necessity arises from the structure of quantification and predication rather than from truth-functional combination alone.

background_cases: From 'If it is raining, the streets are wet' and the observation that it is raining, we conclude the streets are wet; the implication functions as expected.
From 'If a number is divisible by four, it is divisible by two' and the fact that eight is divisible by four, we conclude eight is divisible by two; again, implication works correctly.
From 'If the switch is on, the light is on' and the fact that the switch is on, we infer the light is on; the conditional captures a genuine connection.
definition: A conditional 'if P then Q' is true if and only if (i) there exists a genuine connection between P and Q, (ii) whenever P obtains, Q obtains as a consequence, and (iii) denying Q while affirming P leads to an absurdity.
conditions: genuine connection between P and Q
whenever P obtains Q obtains
denying Q while affirming P is absurd
counterexample: The conditional 'If five is even, then London is the capital of France' has a false antecedent. Denying the consequent while affirming the antecedent yields the conjunction 'five is even and London is not the capital of France,' which is not absurd — it is merely false. There is no genuine connection between the evenness of five and the capital of France. Yet in classical logic the conditional is true, because the antecedent is false.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved conditionals with true antecedents and a causal or logical connection between antecedent and consequent. The definition extracted the notion of genuine connection from these cases, but classical logic evaluates the material conditional purely truth-functionally, rendering it true whenever the antecedent is false, regardless of any connection.
passage:
Consider three cases of implication that function as we expect. From 'If it is raining, the streets are wet' and the observation that it is raining, we conclude the streets are wet. From 'If a number is divisible by four, it is divisible by two' and the fact that eight is divisible by four, we conclude that eight is divisible by two. From 'If the switch is on, the light is on' and the fact that the switch is on, we infer the light is on. In each case there is a genuine connection between antecedent and consequent, the obtaining of the antecedent brings about the consequent, and denying the consequent while affirming the antecedent would be absurd. One might define: a conditional 'if P then Q' is true if and only if there is a genuine connection between P and Q, whenever P obtains Q obtains, and denying Q while affirming P leads to an absurdity.

But consider the conditional 'If five is even, then London is the capital of France.' The antecedent is false: five is not even. In classical logic, a material conditional with a false antecedent is true regardless of the consequent. The conditional is therefore true. Yet there is no genuine connection between the evenness of five and the status of London. Denying the consequent while affirming the antecedent — asserting that five is even and London is not the capital of France — is not absurd but merely a conjunction of falsehoods. None of the three conditions is satisfied, yet classical logic pronounces the conditional true. The background cases were all instances where the antecedent was true and a real connection obtained. The definition, drawn from these congenial cases, failed to anticipate the behaviour of the material conditional when the antecedent is false — a behaviour that many logicians have regarded as paradoxical.

background_cases: From 'All men are mortal' and 'Socrates is a man,' we deduce 'Socrates is mortal'; the conclusion follows necessarily.
From 'All even numbers are divisible by two' and 'Fourteen is even,' we deduce 'Fourteen is divisible by two'; again, the deduction is certain.
From 'No reptiles are mammals' and 'All snakes are reptiles,' we deduce 'No snakes are mammals'; the conclusion is guaranteed by the premises.
definition: A piece of reasoning is a deduction if and only if (i) the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises, (ii) the reasoning proceeds from universal premises to a particular conclusion, and (iii) the conclusion contains no information not already present in the premises.
conditions: conclusion follows necessarily
proceeds from universal to particular
conclusion adds no new information
counterexample: From the premises 'If it is raining then the match is cancelled' and 'It is raining,' one deduces 'The match is cancelled.' The conclusion follows necessarily, yet neither premise is a universal generalisation — one is a conditional about a particular event, the other a particular observation. The second condition, requiring universal-to-particular reasoning, is not met, though the inference is plainly deductive.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all syllogisms proceeding from universal premises. The definition abstracted the direction of reasoning from these paradigm cases, but deduction is characterised by the necessity of the inference, not by the logical form of the premises; deductive arguments from particular premises are entirely possible.
passage:
Consider three paradigmatic deductions. From 'All men are mortal' and 'Socrates is a man,' we deduce that Socrates is mortal. From 'All even numbers are divisible by two' and 'Fourteen is even,' we deduce that fourteen is divisible by two. From 'No reptiles are mammals' and 'All snakes are reptiles,' we deduce that no snakes are mammals. In each case the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises, the reasoning moves from universal premises to a particular or restricted conclusion, and the conclusion seems to contain nothing beyond what was already implicit in the premises. One might therefore define: a piece of reasoning is a deduction if and only if the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises, the reasoning proceeds from universal premises to a particular conclusion, and the conclusion contains no information not already present in the premises.

But consider the following argument: 'If it is raining, then the match is cancelled. It is raining. Therefore, the match is cancelled.' The conclusion follows necessarily from the premises; this is an instance of modus ponens, one of the most elementary forms of deductive reasoning. Yet neither premise is a universal generalisation. The first is a conditional about a particular event — this rain and this match — and the second is a particular observation about the weather. The second condition of the definition, requiring that deduction proceed from universal premises to a particular conclusion, is not satisfied. The background cases were all syllogisms — arguments with universal major premises — and the definition was drawn from that special form. But deduction is not defined by the form of its premises; it is defined by the necessity of the connection between premises and conclusion. A deductive argument may proceed from particulars to particulars, from particulars to universals, or indeed in any direction, provided the conclusion cannot be false when the premises are true.

background_cases: In propositional logic, every valid formula can be derived from the axioms using modus ponens; the system is complete.
In the monadic predicate calculus, every valid formula is derivable; the system is again complete.
In the Boolean algebra of sets, every identity that holds in all models can be proved from the axioms; completeness holds.
definition: A formal system is adequate if and only if (i) it is consistent, meaning no contradiction is derivable, (ii) it is complete, meaning every truth expressible in its language is a theorem, and (iii) every derivation within it terminates in a finite number of steps.
conditions: consistent
complete
every derivation terminates finitely
counterexample: First-order Peano arithmetic is consistent (assuming the standard view) and remarkably powerful. But by Goedel's incompleteness theorem, it is not complete: there are arithmetical truths expressible in its language that are not theorems. Furthermore, the question of whether a given formula is a theorem is not decidable in general — there is no guarantee that a search for a derivation will terminate. The second and third conditions both fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all decidable systems with relatively simple structure. The definition extrapolated from these well-behaved cases to a general account, but systems powerful enough to express arithmetic lose both completeness and decidability, as Goedel and Church respectively demonstrated.
passage:
Consider three formal systems in which everything works as one might wish. In propositional logic, every valid formula can be derived from the axioms by the repeated application of modus ponens; the system is complete. In the monadic predicate calculus — predicate logic restricted to one-place predicates — every valid formula is again derivable. In the Boolean algebra of sets, every identity that holds in all models can be proved from the axioms. One might define: a formal system is adequate if and only if it is consistent (no contradiction is derivable), it is complete (every truth expressible in its language is a theorem), and every derivation within it terminates in a finite number of steps.

But consider first-order Peano arithmetic, the standard formalisation of elementary number theory. This system is, on the standard view, consistent. It is enormously powerful, capable of expressing a vast range of arithmetical propositions. Yet Goedel's first incompleteness theorem shows that it is not complete: there exist arithmetical propositions expressible in the language of the system that are true but not provable within the system. The second condition fails. Moreover, the question of whether a given formula of arithmetic is a theorem is not, in general, decidable. Church's theorem establishes that there is no algorithm that will, for every formula, determine in a finite number of steps whether or not it is derivable. The third condition fails as well. The background cases — propositional logic, the monadic predicate calculus, Boolean algebra — were all decidable systems of limited expressive power. The definition, drawn from these well-behaved examples, assumed that consistency, completeness, and decidability could be simultaneously achieved. The deep results of Goedel and Church show that for any system rich enough to formalise elementary arithmetic, this assumption is false.

background_cases: 'It is raining and it is not raining' is a contradiction; no possible situation makes it true.
'The door is open and the door is closed' is contradictory, for a single door cannot be simultaneously open and closed.
'All swans are white and some swans are not white' is a contradiction; the universal and particular claims cannot both hold.
definition: A pair of propositions is contradictory if and only if (i) the two propositions cannot both be true, (ii) the two propositions cannot both be false, and (iii) the negation of one is logically equivalent to the other.
conditions: cannot both be true
cannot both be false
negation of one equivalent to the other
counterexample: The propositions 'All birds fly' and 'Some birds do not fly' cannot both be true: if all birds fly, then there are no non-flying birds. They cannot both be false, for if it is false that all birds fly, then some birds do not fly, making the second proposition true. They are contradictories in the classical sense. But the propositions 'All birds fly' and 'No birds fly' are contraries, not contradictories: they cannot both be true, but they can both be false (if some but not all birds fly). The definition correctly excludes contraries from the class of contradictories, yet many speakers loosely call contrary propositions 'contradictory.'
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved propositions that exhausted the logical space: exactly one of the pair was true in every possible situation. The definition captured this exhaustiveness, but ordinary usage conflates contradiction with mere contrariety, where both propositions may be false. The definition, properly applied, is narrower than common parlance suggests.
passage:
Consider three cases of contradiction. The conjunction 'It is raining and it is not raining' cannot be true in any possible circumstance; it is a flat contradiction. The claim that a particular door is both open and closed is similarly impossible: the two states are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive (setting aside intermediate positions). The universal claim 'All swans are white' and the particular claim 'Some swans are not white' cannot both be true. From these cases one might define: a pair of propositions is contradictory if and only if the two cannot both be true, cannot both be false, and the negation of one is logically equivalent to the other.

But consider the propositions 'All birds fly' and 'No birds fly.' These cannot both be true: if all birds fly, then it is false that no birds fly. So the first condition is met. Yet they can both be false, for if some but not all birds fly, then 'All birds fly' is false and 'No birds fly' is also false. The second condition fails. These propositions are contraries, not contradictories. The definition correctly excludes them: genuine contradictories must exhaust the logical space, leaving no room for both to be false. Yet ordinary usage frequently conflates contrariety with contradiction. One says, loosely, that two opposing claims 'contradict' one another when in fact they are merely contrary. The background cases all involved propositions that divided the logical space cleanly in two; the definition, drawn from these clean cases, identifies a narrower and more precise notion than the one that governs everyday speech. The distinction between contradiction and contrariety, though elementary, is indispensable for rigorous reasoning.

background_cases: The ad hominem fallacy: attacking the character of the arguer rather than the argument — e.g., dismissing a scientist's findings because he is personally disagreeable.

The straw man fallacy: misrepresenting an opponent's position and then refuting the misrepresentation — e.g., attributing to a moderate reformer the view that all existing institutions should be abolished.
The appeal to authority: accepting a proposition because a famous person endorses it, regardless of whether the person has relevant expertise.
definition: An argument is fallacious if and only if (i) the error can be attributed to the arguer's intention to deceive, (ii) the fallacy is immediately recognisable to a competent logician, and (iii) the conclusion is false.
conditions: error attributable to intention to deceive
immediately recognisable to competent logician
conclusion is false
counterexample: A mathematician who offers an invalid proof of a true theorem commits a fallacy — his reasoning is defective — but his conclusion happens to be true. He had no intention to deceive; he genuinely believed his proof was correct. And the error, if it involves a subtle misuse of transfinite induction, may not be immediately recognisable even to a competent logician. All three conditions fail, yet the argument is fallacious.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all rhetorical fallacies committed in debate, where deceptive intent is plausible, the errors are well-known, and the conclusions are typically dubious. The definition abstracted intent, recognisability, and falsity of conclusion as essential, but mathematical fallacies show that an argument may be fallacious without any deceptive intent, with errors that elude expert scrutiny, and with a conclusion that is nonetheless true.
passage:
Consider three fallacies. The ad hominem, which attacks the arguer rather than the argument. The straw man, which misrepresents the opponent's position. The appeal to authority, which substitutes fame for evidence. In each case, the arguer may be suspected of an intention to mislead, the error is readily identified by anyone trained in logic, and the conclusion is typically false or unwarranted. From these cases one might define fallacy: an argument is fallacious if and only if the error can be attributed to the arguer's intention to deceive, the fallacy is immediately recognisable to a competent logician, and the conclusion is false.

But this definition, drawn from the rhetorical abuses of public debate, proves too narrow. Consider a mathematician who publishes what he believes to be a valid proof of a true theorem — say, a result in number theory that has been confirmed computationally but not yet proved. His proof contains a subtle error: a misapplication of transfinite induction at a critical juncture. The error is so subtle that it escapes the notice of the referees and is discovered only years later by a specialist. The mathematician had no intention to deceive — he believed sincerely that his proof was sound. The error is not immediately recognisable; it eluded competent logicians for years. And the conclusion is true — the theorem has been established by other means. All three conditions fail. Yet the argument is fallacious: the reasoning is invalid, and the conclusion, though true, does not follow from the premisses as stated. The background cases were rhetorical fallacies, and the definition mistook the accidental features of rhetorical abuse — deceptive intent, easy recognition, false conclusions — for the essential features of fallacy, which is simply defective reasoning, regardless of the arguer's motive, the visibility of the error, or the truth-value of the conclusion.

background_cases: If the temperature drops below zero, the pond will freeze: here the antecedent causally produces the consequent.
If the barometer falls sharply, a storm is approaching: the antecedent is a reliable indicator of the consequent.
If the litmus paper turns red, the solution is acidic: the antecedent is a diagnostic sign of the consequent.
definition: A conditional 'if P then Q' is true if and only if (i) P is causally or evidentially connected to Q, (ii) the connection holds in all normal circumstances, and (iii) the conditional is falsifiable — there is some possible observation that would show it to be false.
conditions: P causally or evidentially connected to Q
connection holds in all normal circumstances
conditional is falsifiable
counterexample: The conditional 'If 7 is prime, then 7 is odd' is true, but there is no causal or evidential connection between 7's being prime and 7's being odd — both are independent mathematical facts. The connection does not hold 'in normal circumstances' in any empirical sense, since it is not an empirical matter at all. And the conditional is not falsifiable by any possible observation. All three conditions fail, yet the conditional is true.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all empirical conditionals involving causal or evidential connections in the natural world. The definition treated causal or evidential linkage, empirical robustness, and falsifiability as essential, but mathematical and logical conditionals are true by virtue of the logical relations among their components, without any causal connection or empirical content.
passage:
Consider three conditionals. If the temperature drops below zero, the pond will freeze: the antecedent causes the consequent. If the barometer falls, a storm approaches: the antecedent is evidence for the consequent. If litmus paper turns red, the solution is acidic: the antecedent is a diagnostic sign. From these cases one might define the conditional: 'if P then Q' is true if and only if P is causally or evidentially connected to Q, the connection holds in all normal circumstances, and the conditional is falsifiable.

But this definition, drawn from empirical conditionals, fails when applied to the conditionals of mathematics and logic. Consider the conditional 'If 7 is prime, then 7 is odd.' This is true. But there is no causal connection between 7's being prime and 7's being odd; causal relations do not obtain among mathematical facts. Nor is there an evidential connection in the ordinary sense — one does not infer the oddness of 7 from its primality, or vice versa. The connection does not hold 'in normal circumstances' in any empirical sense, because the truth of the conditional is not an empirical matter; it is a consequence of the definitions of primality and oddness. And the conditional is not falsifiable by any possible observation; no experiment could reveal a case in which 7 is prime but not odd. All three conditions fail. The background cases were empirical conditionals grounded in causal and evidential relations. The definition mistook these empirical features for the essence of the conditional, excluding the mathematical and logical conditionals that are true by virtue of the relations among their terms rather than by any connection in the natural world.

background_cases: The proposition 'Snow is white' — a simple declarative claim about the colour of a natural substance.
The proposition 'Paris is the capital of France' — a factual claim about a political arrangement.
The proposition 'The Earth revolves around the Sun' — an empirical claim about the solar system.
definition: An utterance expresses a proposition if and only if (i) it describes a state of affairs in the world, (ii) it can be verified or falsified by empirical observation, and (iii) it is expressible in a single declarative sentence.
conditions: describes a state of affairs in the world
verifiable or falsifiable by observation
expressible in single declarative sentence
counterexample: The proposition 'There is no greatest prime number' does not describe a state of affairs in the empirical world — it concerns the abstract domain of mathematics. It cannot be verified or falsified by observation; its truth is established by proof. Yet it is a proposition — it is either true or false (and in fact true), and it is the content of Euclid's famous theorem. The first and second conditions fail, yet the utterance expresses a genuine proposition.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all empirical propositions about observable states of affairs. The definition treated empirical describability and observational verifiability as essential, but mathematical propositions concern abstract objects and are established by proof rather than observation, yet they are no less propositional for that.
passage:
Consider three propositions. 'Snow is white' — a claim about a natural substance. 'Paris is the capital of France' — a claim about a political fact. 'The Earth revolves around the Sun' — a claim about the physical world. Each describes a state of affairs, each can be verified by observation, and each is expressible in a single declarative sentence. From these cases one might define: an utterance expresses a proposition if and only if it describes a state of affairs in the world, it can be verified or falsified by empirical observation, and it is expressible in a single declarative sentence.

But this definition, drawn from empirical claims, proves too narrow. Consider the utterance 'There is no greatest prime number.' This is a proposition — it is either true or false, and it is in fact true: Euclid proved it more than two thousand years ago by showing that any finite list of primes can be extended. The proposition is the content of one of the most celebrated theorems in the history of mathematics. Yet it does not describe a state of affairs in the empirical world; there are no prime numbers to be observed in nature, and the proposition concerns the abstract structure of the number system. It cannot be verified or falsified by observation; no telescope, no microscope, no instrument of any kind can reveal whether there is a greatest prime. Its truth is established by deductive proof from the axioms of arithmetic. The first and second conditions fail. The background cases were all empirical propositions, and the definition mistook empirical content and observational access for essential features of propositionhood. But mathematical propositions are genuine propositions — they have determinate truth-values and are the objects of proof and refutation — and their truth is independent of any empirical observation.

background_cases: The analogy between the heart and a pump: both move fluid through a system of channels, both operate by rhythmic contraction, and both serve to sustain the functioning of a larger system.
The analogy between the eye and a camera: both admit light through an aperture, both focus it through a lens, and both produce an image on a sensitive surface.
definition: An analogy is cogent if and only if (i) the two compared things share structural features, (ii) the structural similarities are greater than the differences, and (iii) the analogy suggests a specific, testable prediction about the less well-known case.
conditions: compared things share structural features
similarities greater than differences
analogy suggests specific testable prediction
counterexample: The analogy between a society and an organism — each has interdependent parts, each requires coordination, each must adapt to survive — has been immensely fruitful in social thought. Yet it does not suggest a specific, testable prediction about society; its value lies not in prediction but in illumination, in making visible certain features of social life that would otherwise be overlooked. The third condition fails, yet the analogy is among the most cogent and influential in the history of ideas.
abductive_insight: The background cases were both scientific analogies that yielded specific functional predictions. The definition treated predictive yield as essential, but the organism-society analogy shows that an analogy may be cogent and illuminating by revealing structural parallels that deepen understanding, even when it generates no testable predictions.
passage:
Consider two analogies. The heart is like a pump: both move fluid, both work by contraction, both sustain a larger system. The eye is like a camera: both admit light, both focus through a lens, both produce an image. Each analogy shares structural features, the similarities outweigh the differences, and each suggests testable predictions — that the heart, like a pump, will fail if its valves are damaged, or that the eye, like a camera, will produce a blurred image if its lens is deformed. From these cases one might define cogent analogy: an analogy is cogent if and only if the compared things share structural features, the similarities are greater than the differences, and the analogy suggests a specific, testable prediction.

But this definition, drawn from scientific analogies, is too restrictive. Consider the analogy between a society and an organism. This analogy — explored by thinkers from Plato to Spencer — holds that society, like an organism, is composed of interdependent parts that must function in harmony for the whole to survive. Just as the organs of the body serve different functions but contribute to a common life, so the classes and institutions of society perform different roles but sustain the common good. The analogy shares structural features, and one might argue that the similarities are considerable. But it does not suggest a specific, testable prediction about society. One cannot deduce from the analogy that a particular social reform will succeed or fail. Its value lies in illumination rather than prediction — it makes visible certain features of social order, such as functional interdependence and the need for coordination, that might otherwise be overlooked. The third condition fails. The background cases were scientific analogies valued for their predictive power. The definition mistook predictive yield for the sole mark of cogency, excluding the analogies whose contribution lies in deepening understanding rather than in generating testable hypotheses.

background_cases: The proposition 'No bachelor is married' is necessarily true: its truth follows from the meanings of the terms and cannot be denied without contradiction.
The proposition 'All triangles have three sides' is necessarily true: it is a consequence of the definition of a triangle.
definition: A proposition is necessary if and only if (i) its truth is guaranteed by the meanings of its constituent terms, (ii) it is knowable without recourse to experience, and (iii) it conveys no information about the empirical world.
conditions: truth guaranteed by meanings of terms
knowable without recourse to experience
conveys no information about empirical world
counterexample: The proposition 'Water is H2O' is widely regarded as necessarily true — water could not have been anything other than H2O — yet its truth is not guaranteed by the meanings of the terms (one can understand the word 'water' without knowing its chemical composition), it is not knowable without recourse to experience (it was discovered by empirical chemistry), and it conveys genuine information about the empirical world. All three conditions fail, yet the proposition is necessary.
abductive_insight: The background cases were both analytic truths whose necessity derives from the meanings of their terms. background_cases: The liar paradox — 'This sentence is false' — is self-referential, generates a contradiction, and has resisted resolution for millennia. It is a paradigmatic paradox.

Consider three celebrated paradoxes. The liar: 'This sentence is false,' which refers to itself and generates a contradiction whether one assigns it truth or falsity. Each involves self-reference, each generates contradiction, and each has proven extraordinarily resistant to resolution. One might extract a definition: a problem is paradoxical if and only if it involves self-reference, generates an outright contradiction, and cannot be resolved by restricting the terms.

But one must consider the ship of Theseus. A ship has every one of its planks replaced, one at a time, over the course of many years, until no original timber remains. The old planks, meanwhile, are carefully preserved and reassembled into a ship of the same design. There are now two ships — the one in the harbour with all new planks, and the one in the dockyard reassembled from the old. Which is the original ship of Theseus? The problem is paradoxical: our ordinary concept of identity over time appears to yield conflicting verdicts, and philosophers have debated the question since antiquity. Yet there is no self-reference; the ship does not refer to itself. There is no outright logical contradiction; it is not the case that some proposition must be both true and false. And the problem admits of proposed resolutions — one may distinguish numerical from qualitative identity, or appeal to the continuity of form, or deny that identity is determinate in such cases. All three conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were drawn entirely from the domain of self-referential logical puzzles, and the definition abstracted their shared features as essential to paradoxicality. The ship of Theseus is such a case, and the definition, modelled on too narrow a range of examples, cannot accommodate it.

background_cases: The property of being a prime number is definable in the language of arithmetic: one can write a formula that is true of exactly the prime numbers and no others.

The definition abstracted immediacy and transparency to competent speakers as necessary conditions because both held in every case. But entailment encompasses vastly more than analytic unpacking. Mathematical entailments may be extraordinarily long, requiring chains of reasoning that span hundreds of pages, and their recognition may elude the most expert practitioners for centuries. The definition mistook the features of a narrow class of definitional or analytic entailments for the features of entailment as such.

Consider three entailments. 'John is a bachelor' entails 'John is unmarried' — the conclusion unpacks the meaning of the premiss, and anyone who grasps the concept of bachelorhood recognises the entailment at once. 'The figure is a triangle' entails 'The figure has three sides' — again, the recognition is immediate. 'Today is Tuesday' entails 'Today is a weekday' — the conclusion follows directly from the premiss and the structure of the week. In each case the entailment is transparent to any competent speaker and requires no chain of reasoning. One might define entailment: P entails Q if and only if it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, anyone who understands the terms can recognise the entailment, and the recognition is immediate.

But consider the relationship between the axioms of arithmetic and Goldbach's conjecture — the proposition that every even integer greater than two is expressible as the sum of two primes. If the conjecture is true, and if it follows from the axioms, then the axioms entail it: there is no model of the axioms in which the conjecture is false. Every competent mathematician understands the terms — 'even integer,' 'prime,' 'sum.' Yet the entailment, if it exists, is anything but immediately recognisable. The conjecture has been neither proved nor refuted for nearly three centuries; the greatest mathematicians have attempted the proof and failed. If and when the entailment is demonstrated, the proof will require a long and intricate chain of reasoning — quite possibly one of extraordinary subtlety and length. The third condition of the definition fails spectacularly. The background cases were all analytic entailments, in which the conclusion was implicit in the very meaning of the premiss. The definition generalised from these transparent cases to all entailment, mistaking the immediacy and conceptual accessibility of analytic entailments for necessary features of the entailment relation itself. But entailment is a logical relation, not a psychological one, and it may hold even when its recognition demands centuries of collective intellectual effort.

background_cases: The axioms of Euclidean geometry — the five postulates together with the common notions — are consistent, as demonstrated by the existence of the Euclidean plane as a model. All axioms are intuitively evident.
The axioms of elementary group theory are consistent, as demonstrated by the existence of concrete groups — the integers under addition, for instance. The axioms are simple and transparent.

The Peano axioms for arithmetic are consistent, as demonstrated (on the assumption of set-theoretic consistency) by the standard model of the natural numbers. The axioms formalise intuitively obvious properties of the natural numbers.
definition: A set of axioms S is consistent if and only if (i) S has a model, (ii) each axiom in S is intuitively evident, and (iii) the consistency of S can be proved within S itself.
conditions: S has a model
each axiom in S is intuitively evident
the consistency of S can be proved within S itself
counterexample: Consider Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory with the axiom of choice (ZFC). ZFC is widely held to be consistent — the cumulative hierarchy of sets provides a model, at least informally. Yet several of its axioms — the axiom of replacement, the axiom of choice, the power set axiom — are far from intuitively evident; they have been subjects of philosophical dispute since their introduction. Moreover, by Goedel's second incompleteness theorem, if ZFC is consistent, it cannot prove its own consistency. The second and third conditions both fail, yet ZFC is the standard foundational theory of mathematics and is regarded as consistent.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Euclidean geometry, group theory, Peano arithmetic — were all theories whose axioms strike most mathematicians as intuitively obvious, and whose consistency, in certain informal senses, appears self-evident. The definition abstracted intuitive evidence and internal provability of consistency as necessary conditions because both seemed to hold in the simple cases examined. But Goedel's second incompleteness theorem shows that no sufficiently strong consistent theory can prove its own consistency, and many consistent theories — including the foundational theory of modern mathematics — have axioms that are not intuitively evident. The definition was drawn from a sample of simple, well-understood theories and cannot accommodate the more powerful theories upon which mathematical practice actually rests. Consistency is a model-theoretic and proof-theoretic property; it is independent of the intuitive evidence of axioms and cannot, for strong theories, be established from within.

Consider three sets of axioms generally regarded as consistent. The axioms of Euclidean geometry, whose consistency is demonstrated by the Euclidean plane; each axiom — that a straight line may be drawn between any two points, that all right angles are equal, and so forth — is intuitively evident. The axioms of group theory, whose consistency is demonstrated by the integers under addition; the axioms are simple and their truth in this model is transparent. The Peano axioms, whose consistency is demonstrated by the standard model of the natural numbers; the axioms formalise properties of the natural numbers that seem self-evident. One might extract a definition: a set of axioms is consistent if and only if it has a model, each axiom is intuitively evident, and the consistency can be proved within the theory itself.

But one must consider Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory with the axiom of choice — ZFC — the standard foundation of modern mathematics. ZFC is held to be consistent; the cumulative hierarchy of sets provides a model, at least at the level of informal mathematical practice. Yet several of its axioms are far from intuitively evident. The axiom of replacement — that the image of a set under any definable function is itself a set — is a principle whose warrant requires sophisticated set-theoretic reasoning, and it has been questioned by mathematicians of considerable standing. The axiom of choice — that for any collection of non-empty sets there exists a selection function — has been the subject of debate since Zermelo first stated it. These are not principles that strike the uninitiated mind as self-evident. The second condition fails. More decisively, Goedel's second incompleteness theorem, published in 1931, demonstrates that if ZFC is consistent, it cannot prove its own consistency. The consistency of ZFC, if it obtains, is a fact that the theory itself is powerless to establish. The third condition fails. The background cases were drawn from simple theories with intuitively obvious axioms and, in certain informal senses, self-certifying consistency. The definition generalised from these to all axiom systems, mistaking features of elementary theories for requirements of consistency itself. But consistency is a structural property — the non-derivability of a contradiction — and it is wholly independent of whether the axioms strike anyone as evident or whether the theory can certify its own coherence.

background_cases: The argument 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal' is valid: the premisses are true and the conclusion follows necessarily.
The argument 'All mammals are warm-blooded; all whales are mammals; therefore all whales are warm-blooded' is valid: the premisses are true and the conclusion follows necessarily.
The argument 'No reptiles are mammals; all snakes are reptiles; therefore no snakes are mammals' is valid: the premisses are true and the conclusion follows necessarily.
definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A has exactly two premisses, (ii) both premisses of A are true, and (iii) the conclusion of A follows from the premisses by the form of a categorical syllogism.
conditions: A has exactly two premisses
both premisses of A are true
the conclusion of A follows from the premisses by the form of a categorical syllogism
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'If it is raining, then the streets are wet; it is raining; therefore the streets are wet.' This argument is valid — it is an instance of modus ponens, and there is no possible circumstance in which the premisses are true and the conclusion false. Yet its conclusion does not follow by the form of a categorical syllogism; it follows by a rule of propositional logic — the rule of detachment — that operates upon conditional statements rather than upon categorical propositions. The third condition fails, yet the argument is valid.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all categorical syllogisms — arguments with exactly two premisses drawn from the traditional Aristotelian figures. The definition abstracted the syllogistic form, the two-premiss structure, and the truth of the premisses as essential to validity. But the background cases were drawn from a single corner of deductive logic, and the definition mistook the features of that corner for the features of validity as such. The modus ponens argument reveals that valid arguments may employ propositional rather than categorical logic, and that the syllogistic form is merely one pattern among many that can transmit truth from premisses to conclusion. Moreover, the requirement that premisses be true confuses validity with soundness; an argument may be valid even if its premisses are false, provided that the truth of the premisses would guarantee the truth of the conclusion.

Consider three arguments that are recognised as valid. 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal.' 'All mammals are warm-blooded; all whales are mammals; therefore all whales are warm-blooded.' 'No reptiles are mammals; all snakes are reptiles; therefore no snakes are mammals.' Each argument has two premisses, both true; each conclusion follows from the premisses by the form of a categorical syllogism. From these cases one might extract a definition: an argument is valid if and only if it has exactly two premisses, both premisses are true, and the conclusion follows from the premisses by the form of a categorical syllogism. The definition appears to identify the essential structure of valid reasoning.

But one must consider the argument: 'If it is raining, then the streets are wet; it is raining; therefore the streets are wet.' This argument is valid by any standard account of validity. There is no possible circumstance in which the premisses are true and the conclusion false. The pattern of inference — known as modus ponens, or the rule of detachment — is one of the most fundamental in all of logic. Yet the conclusion does not follow by the form of a categorical syllogism. The first premiss is not a categorical proposition of the form 'All A are B' but a conditional statement, 'If P then Q,' and the inference proceeds by affirming the antecedent rather than by relating categories. The third condition of the definition fails. The same difficulty arises for arguments employing disjunctive syllogism, hypothetical syllogism, reductio ad absurdum, and every other pattern of valid reasoning that lies outside the categorical syllogistic. The background cases were drawn exclusively from the Aristotelian syllogism — the oldest and most familiar form of deductive argument — and the definition mistook the features of that particular form for the essential features of validity itself. Furthermore, the requirement that both premisses be true conflates validity with soundness: the argument 'All fish are mammals; all trout are fish; therefore all trout are mammals' is valid, for the conclusion follows from the premisses with necessity, even though the major premiss is false. Validity is a matter of form, not of truth; and the forms of valid reasoning extend far beyond the categorical syllogism.

background_cases: The liar paradox — 'This sentence is false' — generates a contradiction through self-reference: if true then false, if false then true.
The Grelling-Nelson paradox asks whether the adjective 'heterological' (meaning 'not true of itself') is true of itself, generating a contradiction through self-referential predication.
Russell's paradox asks whether the set of all sets that do not contain themselves contains itself, generating a contradiction through self-referential set membership.
definition: A paradox P arises if and only if (i) P involves a self-referential construction in which a term or expression refers to itself, (ii) P generates a formal logical contradiction of the form 'A and not-A,' and (iii) P can be resolved by restricting or prohibiting the self-referential construction that gives rise to it.
conditions: P involves a self-referential construction in which a term or expression refers to itself
P generates a formal logical contradiction of the form 'A and not-A'
P can be resolved by restricting or prohibiting the self-referential construction that gives rise to it
counterexample: The Sorites paradox — the paradox of the heap — involves no self-reference whatsoever. It proceeds from the premiss that one grain of sand is not a heap, together with the principle that adding a single grain to a non-heap cannot produce a heap, to the conclusion that no quantity of sand, however large, constitutes a heap. The paradox generates not a formal contradiction but an absurd conclusion that conflicts with common sense. And it cannot be resolved merely by restricting self-reference, for it contains none; its source lies in the vagueness of the predicate 'heap.' All three conditions fail, yet the Sorites is one of the most ancient and intractable paradoxes in the philosophical literature.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the liar, Grelling-Nelson, Russell's paradox — were all self-referential paradoxes that produce formal contradictions and that have been addressed, at least in part, by restrictions on self-reference (type theory, hierarchies of languages, axiomatic set theory). The definition abstracted self-reference, formal contradiction, and resolution by restriction as essential to paradox. But the background cases were drawn from a single family of semantic and set-theoretic paradoxes, and the definition mistook the features of that family for the features of paradox as such. The Sorites paradox reveals that paradoxes may arise from vagueness rather than self-reference, may produce absurd conclusions rather than formal contradictions, and may resist resolution by any simple prohibition. The definition confused one important source of paradox with the concept of paradox itself.
passage:
Consider three paradoxes that have exercised logicians for many years. The liar paradox — 'This sentence is false' — involves a sentence that refers to itself and generates a contradiction: if the sentence is true then it is false, and if false then true. The Grelling-Nelson paradox asks whether the adjective 'heterological,' defined as an adjective that does not describe itself, is heterological; self-referential predication produces a contradiction. Russell's paradox asks whether the set of all sets that do not contain themselves contains itself; again, a self-referential construction yields a contradiction. From these cases one might extract a definition: a paradox arises when a self-referential construction generates a formal logical contradiction, and it can be resolved by restricting or prohibiting the self-referential construction. The background cases support all three conditions.

But one must consider the Sorites paradox, attributed to Eubulides of Miletus in the fourth century before Christ. Suppose one has a single grain of sand. A single grain of sand is not a heap. Now add one grain. Two grains of sand are not a heap. Add another. Three grains are not a heap. The principle seems unassailable: if n grains of sand do not constitute a heap, then n plus one grains do not constitute a heap either, for a single grain cannot make the difference between a non-heap and a heap. Yet if one applies this principle repeatedly, one is driven to the conclusion that ten thousand grains of sand do not constitute a heap, and indeed that no quantity of sand, however vast, constitutes a heap — a conclusion manifestly at odds with common sense and ordinary usage. The Sorites paradox involves no self-reference. No sentence refers to itself; no set is asked to contain itself; no predicate is applied to its own name. The paradox generates not a formal contradiction of the form 'A and not-A' but rather an absurd conclusion — the denial of heaps — that conflicts violently with what everyone knows to be the case. And it cannot be resolved by restricting self-reference, for there is none to restrict. The source of the paradox lies in the vagueness of the predicate 'heap' and in the assumption that vague predicates must satisfy the principle that a single grain cannot make a relevant difference. The background cases were drawn from a specific family of self-referential paradoxes, and the definition mistook the features of that family — self-reference, formal contradiction, amenability to resolution by restriction — for the essential features of paradox as such, thereby excluding one of the oldest and most illuminating paradoxes in the history of logic.

background_cases: 'All cats are mammals' entails 'Some mammals are cats,' for if the former is true, the latter must be true as well — one cannot have all cats being mammals without at least some mammals being cats.

'John is taller than Peter and Peter is taller than James' entails 'John is taller than James,' for the transitivity of the taller-than relation guarantees the truth of the conclusion given the truth of the premiss.
'The number n is a prime greater than 2' entails 'n is odd,' for every prime greater than 2 is odd, and the truth of the premiss necessitates the truth of the conclusion.
definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) P and Q concern the same subject matter, (ii) the truth of Q can be established by extracting information already contained in P, and (iii) Q is less general than P — that is, Q says less than P does.
conditions: P and Q concern the same subject matter
the truth of Q can be established by extracting information already contained in P
Q is less general than P — that is, Q says less than P does
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'It is raining and the temperature is below freezing.' This conjunction entails 'Either it is raining or the sun is shining,' for the truth of the conjunction guarantees the truth of the disjunction — if it is raining and below freezing, then a fortiori it is raining, and if it is raining, then the disjunction 'it is raining or the sun is shining' is true. Yet the conclusion introduces the subject of sunshine, which is not contained in the premiss. The truth of the conclusion is not established by extracting information from the premiss but by a logical operation — disjunction introduction — that adds content. And the conclusion is more general than the premiss, not less; it is weaker, not stronger. All three conditions fail, yet the entailment holds.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances in which the conclusion was obtained by extracting or narrowing information already present in the premiss — drawing out a particular from a universal, applying transitivity, or deducing a property from a classification. The definition abstracted shared subject matter, information extraction, and decreased generality as essential to entailment. But the background cases represented one natural and intuitive pattern of entailment — the pattern in which the conclusion says less than the premiss. Disjunction introduction reveals that entailment may proceed in the opposite direction: from a stronger, more specific premiss to a weaker, more general conclusion that introduces new subject matter. The definition confused one direction of logical consequence with the whole of entailment, overlooking the fact that any proposition entails any weaker proposition, regardless of whether the weaker proposition shares its subject matter or is 'contained' in it in any intuitive sense.
passage:
Consider three instances of entailment. The proposition 'All cats are mammals' entails 'Some mammals are cats': the particular is contained within the universal, and one draws it out, as it were, by attending to what is already asserted. The conjunction 'John is taller than Peter and Peter is taller than James' entails 'John is taller than James': the transitivity of the taller-than relation allows one to extract a consequence that is, in a clear sense, already implicit in the premiss. The proposition 'The number n is a prime greater than 2' entails 'n is odd': the property of oddness is contained within the property of being prime and greater than 2, and one establishes the conclusion by drawing upon what the premiss already tells us. From these cases one might extract a definition: a proposition entails another if and only if the two concern the same subject matter, the conclusion is established by extracting information already contained in the premiss, and the conclusion is less general than the premiss.

But one must consider the following entailment. The proposition 'It is raining and the temperature is below freezing' entails the proposition 'Either it is raining or the sun is shining.' The entailment holds because the conjunction guarantees that it is raining, and the truth of 'it is raining' suffices for the truth of the disjunction 'it is raining or the sun is shining,' regardless of whether the sun is in fact shining. The inference is valid; there is no possible circumstance in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false. Yet the conclusion introduces the topic of sunshine, a subject matter that appears nowhere in the premiss. The truth of the conclusion is not established by extracting information already present in the premiss; it is established by a logical operation — disjunction introduction — that creates a weaker claim by adding an alternative. And the conclusion is not less general than the premiss but more general; it says less, not more, and is satisfied by a wider range of circumstances. All three conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were drawn from entailments in which the conclusion narrows or specifies what the premiss asserts, and the definition mistook this particular pattern for the essence of entailment. What the disjunction case reveals is that entailment is a matter of the necessary preservation of truth, and that truth may be preserved in directions that have nothing to do with the extraction of contained information or the restriction of subject matter.

background_cases: The argument 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal' is valid because the conclusion follows necessarily from the premisses, and both the premisses and the conclusion are in the indicative mood, expressing propositions that are determinately true or false.

The argument 'If it is raining then the streets are wet; it is raining; therefore the streets are wet' is valid because the conclusion follows by modus ponens, and all sentences involved are indicative and truth-evaluable.

The argument 'No reptiles are mammals; all snakes are reptiles; therefore no snakes are mammals' is valid because the conclusion follows by syllogistic reasoning, and all sentences are declarative propositions.
definition: An argument is valid if and only if (i) every sentence in the argument — premisses and conclusion alike — is a declarative, truth-evaluable proposition, (ii) the conclusion follows necessarily from the premisses, and (iii) the argument contains no sentences expressing commands, questions, or wishes.
conditions: every sentence is a declarative, truth-evaluable proposition
the conclusion follows necessarily from the premisses
the argument contains no commands, questions, or wishes
counterexample: Consider the following piece of reasoning in deontic logic: 'One ought to keep one's promises; this is a promise one has made; therefore one ought to keep this promise.' The premisses and the conclusion contain the modal 'ought,' and it is a matter of philosophical dispute whether sentences containing 'ought' are truth-evaluable in the same way as ordinary indicative sentences. On some views, 'One ought to keep one's promises' expresses not a proposition but a norm or a prescription. The first and third conditions may fail, depending upon one's metaethical commitments. Yet the reasoning is valid by any reasonable standard: the conclusion follows necessarily from the premisses in any deontic logic, and any person who accepts the premisses is rationally committed to accepting the conclusion.

The definition abstracted the declarative, truth-evaluable character of the component sentences as a necessary condition of validity because every sentence in the sample was of this kind. But the sample was drawn from a narrow range of factual arguments, and the definition mistook the features of that range for general requirements. The deontic argument reveals that valid reasoning may involve normative sentences whose truth-evaluability is philosophically contested, and that validity — the necessary preservation of some favourable semantic status from premisses to conclusion — is not restricted to the domain of straightforwardly truth-evaluable propositions.

Consider three arguments whose validity is beyond dispute. The syllogism about Socrates and mortality: every sentence is a declarative proposition, and the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of syllogistic inference. But one must consider the following piece of reasoning: 'One ought to keep one's promises; this is a promise one has made; therefore one ought to keep this promise.' The conclusion follows from the premisses with the same necessity as the conclusion of any syllogism. Any person who accepts both premisses is rationally committed to the conclusion; to accept the premisses and reject the conclusion would be a logical error. background_cases: The liar sentence 'This sentence is false' generates a paradox: if it is true then it is false, and if it is false then it is true. The sentence refers to itself.

The Grelling-Nelson paradox concerns the predicate 'heterological,' which applies to adjectives that do not describe themselves: is 'heterological' heterological? The predicate refers to itself.
definition: A paradox arises if and only if (i) a sentence or definition refers to itself, either directly or by reference to a totality of which it is a member, (ii) this self-reference, combined with classical logic, produces a contradiction, and (iii) the paradox is resolvable by restricting or eliminating the self-reference.

self-reference combined with classical logic produces a contradiction
the paradox is resolvable by restricting or eliminating the self-reference
counterexample: Consider the paradox of the preface. An author writes a book containing many assertions and adds, in the preface, the modest disclaimer: 'I am confident that this book contains at least one error.' If all the assertions in the book are true, then the preface statement is false, and the book does contain an error (the preface statement itself). If any assertion is false, then the preface statement is true. But the author has good reason to believe each individual assertion and equally good reason, given human fallibility, to believe that at least one is erroneous. No self-reference is involved — the preface statement refers not to itself but to the other assertions in the book. The paradox is not generated by self-reference but by the tension between individually rational beliefs and their collective inconsistency. The first condition fails, and the paradox is not obviously resolvable by any restriction on self-reference, since no self-reference is present to restrict.

The definition abstracted self-reference as the essential mechanism of paradox because it was present in every case examined. The definition mistook a feature of one historically prominent family of paradoxes for a necessary condition of paradox in general.
passage:
Consider three paradoxes that have shaped the development of modern logic. The liar sentence — 'This sentence is false' — refers to itself and generates a contradiction: if true it is false, if false it is true. Russell's paradox concerns the set of all sets that are not members of themselves; the definition of this set refers to the totality to which it belongs, and a contradiction follows. The Grelling-Nelson paradox concerns the adjective 'heterological,' which applies to adjectives that do not describe themselves; the predicate applies to itself if and only if it does not, and contradiction results. All three paradoxes involve self-reference, and all three are addressed by restricting or stratifying the self-referential construction. One might extract a definition: a paradox arises if and only if a sentence or definition refers to itself, self-reference combined with classical logic yields a contradiction, and the paradox is resolvable by restricting the self-reference.

But one must consider the paradox of the preface. An author writes a book containing a large number of factual assertions — historical dates, scientific measurements, biographical details — each of which she has checked with reasonable care. In the preface she writes: 'Despite my best efforts, I am confident that this book contains at least one error.' She has good reason to believe each individual assertion in the book, for she has verified each one. But she also has good reason, based on her knowledge of human fallibility and the history of scholarly publishing, to believe that at least one of her many assertions is false. These two sets of beliefs are individually rational yet collectively inconsistent: she believes each assertion and also believes that not all of them are true. No self-reference is involved. The preface statement does not refer to itself; it refers to the other assertions in the book. The paradox does not arise from a self-referential loop but from the tension between rational confidence in each individual belief and rational humility about their collective reliability. The first condition of the definition fails entirely, and the standard remedy of restricting self-reference is inapplicable, for there is no self-reference to restrict. The background cases were drawn from the semantic and set-theoretic paradoxes, and the definition abstracted their shared mechanism — self-reference — as the essence of paradox. The preface paradox reveals that paradox may spring from a wholly different source: the rational management of large bodies of individually credible but collectively inconsistent beliefs.

background_cases: 'All dogs are animals' entails 'If Fido is a dog, then Fido is an animal'; the entailment preserves truth and the conclusion concerns a particular instance of the general claim.

'John is taller than Mary and Mary is taller than Peter' entails 'John is taller than Peter'; the entailment preserves truth and the conclusion is a substantive consequence concerning the same individuals.
definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) Q is a substantive consequence of P — that is, Q says something that goes beyond what is explicitly stated in P, (ii) the truth of P guarantees the truth of Q, and (iii) Q is about the same objects or state of affairs as P.

But the conclusion introduces a new topic — Tuesday — that is not mentioned in the premiss, so the third condition fails. Moreover, the conclusion is in one sense less substantive than the premiss rather than more: it is a weaker claim, a disjunction that follows trivially from one of its disjuncts. One might question whether it 'goes beyond' the premiss. Yet the entailment is logically unimpeachable.

But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of well-behaved, intuitively informative inferences. The disjunction-introduction case reveals that entailment may hold between propositions of disparate subject-matter and may produce conclusions that are weaker and less informative than the premiss. Consider three entailments that are uncontroversially valid. 'All dogs are animals' entails 'If Fido is a dog, then Fido is an animal'; the conclusion is a particular instantiation of the general claim and concerns the same subject-matter. 'It is raining and it is cold' entails 'It is raining'; the conclusion is a component of the premiss, less informative but undeniably entailed. 'John is taller than Mary and Mary is taller than Peter' entails 'John is taller than Peter'; the conclusion is a substantive consequence concerning the same individuals. From these cases one might extract a definition: a proposition entails another if and only if the conclusion is a substantive consequence going beyond what is explicitly stated, the truth of the premiss guarantees the truth of the conclusion, and the conclusion concerns the same objects or state of affairs.

But one must consider the entailment from 'It is raining and it is cold' to 'It is raining or it is Tuesday.'
