background_cases: The liar paradox — 'This sentence is false' — generates a contradiction because if the sentence is true, then what it says is the case, and it says it is false, so it is false; and if it is false, then what it says is not the case, so it is not false, and hence true.

Russell's paradox concerns the set of all sets that do not contain themselves: if such a set contains itself, then by its own definition it does not; and if it does not contain itself, then by its own definition it does.
The Burali-Forti paradox concerns the ordinal number of the set of all ordinals: this ordinal must be greater than every ordinal, including itself, which is a contradiction.
definition: A statement or construction is paradoxical if and only if (i) it is formulated within a well-defined formal or linguistic framework, (ii) it leads, by valid reasoning within that framework, to a contradiction, and (iii) the contradiction arises from the self-referential character of the statement or construction — that is, the statement refers to itself or the construction applies to itself.
conditions: It is formulated within a well-defined framework
It leads by valid reasoning to a contradiction
The contradiction arises from self-reference
counterexample: Consider the paradox of the heap — the sorites paradox. The premiss that one grain of sand does not make a heap is formulated within ordinary language, a well-defined (if informal) linguistic framework. Repeated application of the premiss that adding one grain to a non-heap does not produce a heap leads, by valid reasoning, to the conclusion that no amount of sand makes a heap — a contradiction with the evident fact that large piles of sand are heaps. But the paradox involves no self-reference: no premiss refers to itself, and no construction applies to itself. The paradox arises from the vagueness of the predicate 'heap,' not from self-reference. Condition (iii) fails, yet the sorites is one of the oldest and most discussed paradoxes in philosophy.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the liar, Russell's paradox, Burali-Forti — all involved self-reference, and the self-reference was rightly identified as the source of the contradiction in those cases. But what makes a construction paradoxical is not self-reference per se but the generation of a contradiction by apparently valid reasoning from apparently acceptable premisses. Self-reference is one mechanism by which this can occur, but it is not the only one; vagueness, as the sorites shows, is another. The definition has taken a feature common to one family of paradoxes and elevated it to a necessary condition of paradoxicality itself.
passage:
The most celebrated paradoxes of logic and set theory — the liar, Russell's paradox, the Burali-Forti paradox — share a common structure. Each is formulated within a well-defined framework. Each leads, by apparently valid reasoning, to a contradiction. And each owes its paradoxical character to self-reference: the liar sentence speaks about itself, Russell's set is defined in terms of its own membership, the Burali-Forti ordinal measures a collection that includes itself. From these cases one might extract the definition: a statement or construction is paradoxical if and only if it is formulated within a well-defined framework, leads by valid reasoning to a contradiction, and arises from self-reference.

Yet this definition, shaped by the family of self-referential paradoxes, excludes paradoxes that arise from entirely different sources. Consider the sorites — the paradox of the heap. Its premisses are simple: one grain of sand does not make a heap, and adding one grain to something that is not a heap does not make it a heap. These premisses are formulated within ordinary language and appear to be true. Yet repeated application of the second premiss leads, by a chain of valid inferences, to the conclusion that no quantity of sand, however large, constitutes a heap — a conclusion that contradicts the plainest facts of experience. The paradox is genuine: it generates a contradiction from apparently acceptable premisses by apparently valid reasoning. But it involves no self-reference whatever. No premiss speaks about itself; no construction applies to itself. The source of the paradox is the vagueness of the predicate 'heap' — the absence of a sharp boundary between heaps and non-heaps. The definition, by requiring self-reference, excludes the sorites and all paradoxes that arise from vagueness, ambiguity, or other non-self-referential sources. What the background cases had in common was not self-reference but the more general feature of generating contradiction from seemingly unimpeachable premisses. The definition has mistaken a sufficient condition for paradox — self-reference — for a necessary one.

background_cases: The argument 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal' is valid: it is impossible for the premisses to be true and the conclusion false, because the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rule of universal instantiation.

The argument 'If it is raining then the streets are wet; it is raining; therefore the streets are wet' is valid: the conclusion follows from the premisses by modus ponens, and there is no possible circumstance in which both premisses are true while the conclusion is false.

The argument 'All birds have feathers; all creatures with feathers are warm-blooded; therefore all birds are warm-blooded' is valid: the conclusion follows by the transitivity of the universal conditional, and no situation allows the premisses to be true with the conclusion false.
definition: An argument is valid if and only if (i) its conclusion follows from its premisses by a recognised rule of inference, (ii) it is impossible for the premisses to be true and the conclusion false, and (iii) the premisses are relevant to the conclusion.
conditions: Its conclusion follows from its premisses by a recognised rule of inference
It is impossible for the premisses to be true and the conclusion false
The premisses are relevant to the conclusion
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'The moon is made of green cheese (premiss — assume it is true for the sake of the argument); therefore, either the moon is made of green cheese or grass is green.' The conclusion follows from the premiss by the rule of disjunction introduction, and it is impossible for the premiss to be true and the conclusion false. But one might deny that the premiss — concerning lunar composition — is relevant to the conclusion's second disjunct, which concerns the colour of grass. Condition (iii) would then exclude the argument from the class of valid arguments, yet by every standard logical account the argument is valid.
abductive_insight: The background cases all exhibit a natural topical connection between premisses and conclusion, which makes the relevance condition seem innocuous. But disjunction introduction demonstrates that a valid argument need not exhibit topical relevance: the premiss may have nothing to do with one of the disjuncts it logically entails. Validity is a formal property — the impossibility of true premisses with a false conclusion — and relevance is a separate desideratum that the concept of validity does not incorporate.
passage:
The three background cases — Socrates' mortality, the wet streets, and the warm-blooded birds — share a common structure: in each, the premisses bear an obvious topical connection to the conclusion, the conclusion follows by a recognised rule of inference, and it is impossible for the premisses to be true while the conclusion is false. Drawing upon these cases, one might define validity as requiring all three features: derivability by a recognised rule, the impossibility of true premisses with a false conclusion, and the relevance of the premisses to the conclusion.

Yet the relevance condition proves too restrictive. Consider the argument whose sole premiss is 'The moon is made of green cheese' and whose conclusion is 'Either the moon is made of green cheese or grass is green.' The conclusion follows from the premiss by disjunction introduction — a rule of inference as firmly established as modus ponens. And it is impossible for the premiss to be true while the conclusion is false, since a disjunction is true whenever one of its disjuncts is true. But the premiss, which concerns the composition of the moon, bears no topical relevance to the second disjunct, which concerns the colour of grass. If the definition requires relevance, this argument is excluded from the class of valid arguments. Yet it is valid by every standard account. The background cases, it now appears, were misleading in their uniformity: they all happened to exhibit relevance, but relevance is no part of what makes an argument valid. Validity is a purely formal property — the impossibility of truth-preservation failure — and the definition must be purged of the relevance condition.

background_cases: Russell's paradox arises from the set of all sets that do not contain themselves: if it contains itself, then by its defining condition it does not; if it does not contain itself, then by its defining condition it does. The paradox is generated by an unrestricted comprehension axiom.

The Liar paradox arises from the sentence 'This sentence is false': if it is true, then what it says is the case, so it is false; if it is false, then what it says is not the case, so it is true. The paradox is generated by self-referential predication of truth.

Grelling's paradox arises from the predicate 'heterological,' defined as applying to predicates that do not apply to themselves: 'heterological' is heterological if and only if it is not heterological. The paradox is generated by self-application of a semantically defined predicate.
definition: A paradox arises from a definition D if and only if (i) D employs a property that can be meaningfully applied to the very entity it defines, (ii) this self-application generates a contradiction, and (iii) each step of the reasoning leading to the contradiction is individually valid.
conditions: D employs a property that can be meaningfully applied to the very entity it defines
This self-application generates a contradiction
Each step of the reasoning leading to the contradiction is individually valid
counterexample: Consider the Berry paradox: 'the smallest natural number not definable in fewer than twenty syllables.' This phrase appears to define a natural number, and the phrase itself contains fewer than twenty syllables, which generates a contradiction — the number is defined in fewer than twenty syllables by a phrase that says it cannot be so defined. Each step of the reasoning seems individually valid. But the paradox does not arise from self-application in the same sense as Russell's or the Liar: the phrase does not apply a property to itself but rather exploits an ambiguity in the notion of definability. Condition (i) is not clearly satisfied, yet the Berry paradox is a genuine paradox.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve a definition that feeds back upon itself in a direct and structural way — set membership applied to the set being defined, truth applied to the sentence asserting it, heterologicality applied to the predicate so named. The Berry paradox exploits a different mechanism: the vagueness or context-dependence of the notion of definability rather than a straightforward self-application. It appears that self-application is one route to paradox but not the only one, and the definition is too narrow in treating it as the sole mechanism.
passage:
Russell's paradox, the Liar, and Grelling's paradox share a strikingly uniform structure. In each case, a definition employs a property — set membership, truth, heterologicality — that can be meaningfully applied to the very entity the definition introduces. When this self-application is carried out, a contradiction results, and each step of the reasoning is individually beyond reproach. One might therefore suppose that paradoxes of this type arise precisely when a definition permits self-application that generates a contradiction through individually valid steps.

Yet this analysis proves too narrow when we consider the Berry paradox. The phrase 'the smallest natural number not definable in fewer than twenty syllables' appears to define a particular number. The phrase itself contains fewer than twenty syllables, which means it defines — in fewer than twenty syllables — a number that, by hypothesis, cannot be so defined. A contradiction results, and the reasoning seems valid at each step. Yet the paradox does not arise from self-application in the sense captured by condition (i). The phrase does not apply a property to itself in the way that the Liar sentence predicates falsity of itself or the Russell set applies non-self-membership to itself. Rather, it exploits an ambiguity or vagueness in the notion of 'definability' — a semantic notion whose boundaries are insufficiently sharp to sustain the definition without paradox. The background cases, in their structural similarity, suggested that self-application is the essential mechanism of paradox. The Berry case demonstrates that paradoxes may arise from different mechanisms altogether, and the definition must be broadened to accommodate them.

background_cases: From 'All mammals are warm-blooded' and 'All whales are mammals,' the proposition 'All whales are warm-blooded' follows as a logical consequence. The conclusion is necessitated by the premisses in virtue of the transitivity of the universal conditional.

From 'If it is Tuesday then the museum is closed' and 'It is Tuesday,' the proposition 'The museum is closed' follows as a logical consequence. The conclusion is necessitated by the premisses in virtue of the rule of modus ponens.

From 'No reptile is warm-blooded' and 'All lizards are reptiles,' the proposition 'No lizard is warm-blooded' follows as a logical consequence. The conclusion is necessitated by the premisses in virtue of the syllogistic rule governing universal negatives and universal affirmatives.
definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions S if and only if (i) the truth of all propositions in S necessitates the truth of Q, (ii) this necessitation holds in virtue of the logical form of the propositions involved, and (iii) Q can be derived from S by a finite sequence of applications of valid rules of inference.
conditions: The truth of all propositions in S necessitates the truth of Q
This necessitation holds in virtue of the logical form of the propositions involved
Q can be derived from S by a finite sequence of applications of valid rules of inference
counterexample: Consider first-order Peano arithmetic and the proposition known as Goedel's sentence G, which in effect asserts its own unprovability within the system. If Peano arithmetic is consistent, then G is true in the standard model — the truth of the axioms necessitates the truth of G, and this necessitation holds in virtue of formal properties. Yet G cannot be derived from the axioms by any finite sequence of applications of the rules of inference of the system, for G is precisely the sentence whose unprovability within the system is demonstrated. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied while condition (iii) fails, yet G is a logical consequence of the axioms in the semantic sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases are all finitely derivable, which made condition (iii) seem harmless. But Goedel's incompleteness theorem reveals a gap between semantic consequence (truth-preservation across all models) and syntactic derivability (provability by finite proof). The definition conflates these two notions, assuming that whatever is semantically necessitated is syntactically derivable. The cases where this assumption fails are precisely those that reveal the insufficiency of the definition.
passage:
The three background cases suggest that logical consequence involves a triple requirement: the premisses necessitate the conclusion, the necessitation is formal, and the conclusion is derivable by a finite proof. In each case — the warm-blooded whales, the closed museum, the cold-blooded lizards — all three conditions are transparently satisfied, and one might suppose that they jointly capture the concept of logical consequence without remainder.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by a celebrated result in mathematical logic. Consider the axioms of first-order Peano arithmetic and the proposition known as Goedel's sentence, which in effect asserts its own unprovability within the system. If Peano arithmetic is consistent — as we have every reason to suppose — then the truth of the axioms necessitates the truth of Goedel's sentence in the standard model of arithmetic. This necessitation holds in virtue of the formal structure of the axioms and the sentence. Conditions (i) and (ii) are therefore satisfied. But condition (iii) is not: Goedel's incompleteness theorem demonstrates that the sentence cannot be derived from the axioms by any finite sequence of applications of the system's rules of inference. The sentence is true in every model in which the axioms are true, and yet it is not provable from the axioms. The background cases, in their simplicity, concealed a distinction that the Goedel result forces upon us: the distinction between semantic consequence — truth-preservation across models — and syntactic derivability. The definition, by requiring finite derivability as a condition of logical consequence, conflates these two notions and thereby excludes genuine consequences that are semantically necessitated but syntactically unprovable.

background_cases: The proposition 'John is a bachelor' entails 'John is unmarried': it is impossible for the former to be true and the latter false, because being unmarried is part of what it is to be a bachelor.

The proposition 'The figure is a square' entails 'The figure has four sides': it is impossible for something to be a square without having four sides, because having four sides is a necessary condition for being a square.

The proposition 'It is raining and it is cold' entails 'It is raining': it is impossible for a conjunction to be true while one of its conjuncts is false, because the truth of a conjunction requires the truth of each conjunct.
definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, (ii) the impossibility arises from the meanings of the terms involved or from logical form, and (iii) a competent speaker who grasps the meanings of P and Q would recognise the entailment upon reflection.
conditions: It is impossible for P to be true and Q false
The impossibility arises from the meanings of the terms involved or from logical form
A competent speaker who grasps the meanings of P and Q would recognise the entailment upon reflection
counterexample: Consider the proposition P: 'Water is present' and the proposition Q: 'H2O is present.' If water is H2O — as empirical investigation has established — then it is impossible for P to be true and Q false. This impossibility arises from the identity of water with H2O, which is a fact about the nature of the substance rather than about the meanings of the terms. A competent speaker who does not know that water is H2O would not recognise the entailment upon reflection. Condition (iii) fails, yet the entailment holds.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve entailments that are transparent to a competent speaker: anyone who grasps the meaning of 'bachelor' can see that it entails 'unmarried.' But the water/H2O case involves a necessary connection that is discovered empirically rather than grasped through meaning alone. The definition's third condition assumes that all entailments are a priori recognisable, which conflates the a priori with the necessary. Some necessary truths — and hence some entailments — are knowable only a posteriori.
passage:
The entailment from 'bachelor' to 'unmarried,' from 'square' to 'four-sided,' and from a conjunction to its conjuncts share a notable feature: in each case, a competent speaker who grasps the meanings of the terms involved can recognise, upon reflection, that the entailment holds. The impossibility of the premiss being true while the conclusion is false is, as it were, transparent to anyone possessed of the relevant linguistic competence. One might therefore include, as a condition upon entailment, that a competent speaker would recognise the relation upon reflection.

Yet consider the propositions 'Water is present' and 'H2O is present.' Empirical investigation has established that water is identical with H2O. If this identity holds — and it holds necessarily, if it holds at all — then it is impossible for 'Water is present' to be true while 'H2O is present' is false. The entailment is genuine. But a competent speaker of English who does not know the chemical composition of water would not recognise the entailment upon reflection. She might sincerely assert 'Water is present' while denying 'H2O is present,' not because she is confused about logic but because the connection between the two propositions is empirical rather than conceptual. The background cases misled us into thinking that all entailments are a priori transparent, when in fact some necessary connections — and therefore some entailments — are discoverable only through empirical investigation. The definition must be revised to accommodate entailments that hold necessarily but are not recognisable through reflection on meanings alone.

background_cases: The syllogism 'All mammals are warm-blooded; all whales are mammals; therefore all whales are warm-blooded' is valid because there is no possible situation in which the premises are true and the conclusion false, and this truth-preservation can be verified by examining the logical form alone.

The modus ponens argument 'If it is raining then the streets are wet; it is raining; therefore the streets are wet' is valid because the form guarantees that whenever the premises are true the conclusion must be true, regardless of the subject-matter.

The disjunctive syllogism 'Either the butler or the gardener committed the crime; the butler did not commit the crime; therefore the gardener committed the crime' is valid because the logical structure ensures truth-preservation across all possible assignments of truth-values to the component propositions.
definition: An argument is valid if and only if (i) its premises and conclusion are well-formed propositions, (ii) there is no possible assignment of truth-values to the component propositions that makes all premises true and the conclusion false, and (iii) the truth-preservation is a function of the logical form of the argument rather than the particular content of the propositions.
conditions: Premises and conclusion are well-formed propositions
No possible truth-value assignment makes all premises true and the conclusion false
Truth-preservation is a function of logical form
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All bachelors are unmarried men; John is a bachelor; therefore John is an unmarried man.' This argument is truth-preserving in every possible situation — there is no assignment that makes the premises true and the conclusion false. But the truth-preservation does not rest on logical form alone. The argument form is 'All F are G; a is F; therefore a is G,' which is valid. Yet the truth of the first premise is guaranteed not by logic but by the meaning of 'bachelor.' If we replaced 'bachelor' with an arbitrary predicate — say 'All plumbers are unmarried men' — the argument would remain formally valid but the first premise would no longer be necessarily true. The point is that the argument's irresistibility depends partly on semantic content and not solely on form, complicating the third condition.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve arguments whose truth-preservation is transparently a matter of logical form: the syllogism, modus ponens, and disjunctive syllogism are valid in virtue of their structure, irrespective of the content of their component propositions. The definition abstracts from these cases the requirement that validity be a function of form alone. But the bachelor argument reveals a class of arguments whose truth-preservation depends partly on the meaning of non-logical terms — on analytic or definitional truths — and thus occupies a borderland between formal validity and material validity that the definition does not acknowledge.
passage:
When we examine the arguments most confidently classed as valid, we find that their truth-preservation is a matter of logical structure rather than subject-matter. The categorical syllogism preserves truth by virtue of the relations among universal and particular quantifiers. Modus ponens preserves truth by virtue of the conditional connective. The disjunctive syllogism preserves truth by virtue of the logical properties of disjunction and negation. In each case, the particular content of the propositions is irrelevant: any substitution of content that preserves the form will preserve the truth-preservation.

One might therefore define validity by requiring well-formedness, the absence of any falsifying truth-value assignment, and the dependence of truth-preservation on logical form. This definition captures the paradigm cases with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider arguments whose truth-preservation depends upon the meanings of their non-logical terms. Consider the argument: all bachelors are unmarried men; John is a bachelor; therefore John is an unmarried man. The argument is truth-preserving under every possible assignment — there is no situation in which the premises are true and the conclusion false. The argument has the form of a valid syllogism, and it is formally valid. But the irresistibility of the argument does not rest on form alone. The first premise is not an empirical generalisation but a definitional truth: 'bachelor' means 'unmarried man.' The truth-preservation depends in part on this semantic fact. If we substitute an arbitrary predicate for 'bachelor,' the form remains valid, but the first premise becomes a contingent claim that may be false. The background cases all involved truth-preservation that was wholly formal; this case involves truth-preservation that is partly formal and partly semantic. The definition's third condition — that truth-preservation be a function of logical form — excludes or at least obscures this important borderland between the formally and the analytically valid.

background_cases: In classical propositional logic, the conclusion of a valid argument is a logical consequence of the premises in the sense that every truth-value assignment satisfying the premises also satisfies the conclusion; the semantic and syntactic characterisations of consequence coincide perfectly.

In first-order predicate logic, the completeness theorem guarantees that a formula is a semantic consequence of a set of premises if and only if it is derivable from them; the syntactic and semantic notions again coincide.

In standard modal logic S5, the notion of consequence is well-defined: a formula is a consequence of a set of premises if and only if it is true in every world of every model in which all the premises are true; the Kripke semantics provides a clear and unambiguous framework.
definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of propositions P1, ..., Pn if and only if (i) every model that satisfies P1, ..., Pn also satisfies Q, (ii) the consequence relation is compact — that is, if Q is a consequence of an infinite set, it is a consequence of some finite subset, and (iii) the consequence relation is effectively axiomatisable.
conditions: Every model satisfying the premises satisfies Q
The consequence relation is compact
The consequence relation is effectively axiomatisable
counterexample: Consider the standard semantics for second-order logic. The proposition that a structure is a well-ordering of the natural numbers is a semantic consequence of certain second-order axioms: every model of the axioms is indeed a well-ordering. But second-order logical consequence is neither compact nor effectively axiomatisable. There exist sets of second-order sentences whose consequences cannot be captured by any finite subset, and there is no effective procedure for enumerating all second-order consequences. Conditions (ii) and (iii) fail, and yet the consequence relation is perfectly well-defined semantically.
abductive_insight: The background cases — propositional, first-order, and S5 modal logic — all enjoy compactness and effective axiomatisability, and the definition abstracts these properties as necessary conditions for logical consequence. But second-order logic demonstrates that the model-theoretic notion of consequence — truth-preservation across all models — is coherent and important even when compactness and axiomatisability fail. The definition mistakes features of particularly well-behaved logical systems for essential features of the consequence relation itself.
passage:
The logical systems most thoroughly studied in the twentieth century share certain structural properties that invite generalisation. In classical propositional logic, the consequence relation is compact: any consequence of an infinite set of premises is a consequence of some finite subset. The relation is also effectively axiomatisable: there exists a mechanical procedure for enumerating all valid consequences. First-order predicate logic, thanks to the completeness theorem, enjoys the same properties. Standard modal logic S5, with its Kripke semantics, likewise admits of a complete and compact characterisation. In each case, the semantic and syntactic characterisations of consequence align, and the consequence relation is well-behaved in these respects.

One might therefore define logical consequence by requiring truth-preservation across all models, compactness, and effective axiomatisability. This definition captures the paradigm cases faithfully. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by second-order logic under the standard, or full, semantics. In second-order logic, one quantifies not only over individuals but over properties and relations. The consequence relation under the standard semantics is well-defined: a proposition is a consequence of a set of premises if and only if every model satisfying the premises satisfies the proposition. But this consequence relation is neither compact nor effectively axiomatisable. There are second-order theories whose consequences outrun any finite subset of their axioms, and there is no mechanical procedure for listing all second-order validities. The definition would exclude second-order logical consequence from the class of genuine consequence relations — a verdict that most logicians would reject, for the model-theoretic notion of consequence in second-order logic is perfectly coherent and of great mathematical importance. The definition mistakes the well-behaved character of first-order logic for a necessary feature of logical consequence as such.

background_cases: Russell's paradox — consider the set of all sets that do not contain themselves — arises from the unrestricted comprehension principle and is resolved by restricting set formation; the paradox is generated by a single, clearly identifiable principle whose rejection dissolves it.

The Burali-Forti paradox — there is no greatest ordinal number — arises from the assumption that the collection of all ordinals forms a set, and is resolved by denying that assumption; again, a single identifiable principle is at fault.

Cantor's paradox — there is no set of all sets — arises from the assumption that every collection can be treated as a set, and is resolved by distinguishing sets from proper classes; the offending assumption is clearly identifiable.
definition: A paradox is resolved if and only if (i) the set of propositions giving rise to the paradox is identified, (ii) exactly one proposition in the set is shown to be false or ill-formed, and (iii) the rejection of that proposition dissolves the contradiction without generating new difficulties.
conditions: The set of propositions giving rise to the paradox is identified
Exactly one proposition is shown to be false or ill-formed
Rejection of that proposition dissolves the contradiction without new difficulties
counterexample: Consider the sorites paradox. The propositions are: (a) a collection of one hundred thousand grains of sand is a heap; (b) if a collection of n grains is a heap, then a collection of n minus one grains is a heap; (c) a single grain of sand is not a heap. These propositions are jointly inconsistent. Proposition (b) — the tolerance principle — is the natural candidate for rejection. But rejecting it generates a new difficulty: one must now specify a sharp boundary between heap and non-heap, which appears to conflict with the manifest vagueness of the predicate 'heap.' The third condition is not met — the rejection of the offending proposition generates new philosophical difficulties — and yet the paradox demands resolution.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Russell's, Burali-Forti, and Cantor's paradoxes — are all resolved cleanly by the rejection of a single identifiable principle, and the rejection does not generate further difficulties of comparable magnitude. The definition abstracts from these cases the requirement that resolution be clean and complete. But the sorites paradox shows that some paradoxes resist clean resolution: the rejection of the offending proposition dissolves the original contradiction but introduces a new difficulty — the problem of specifying a sharp boundary for a vague predicate — that is itself philosophically intractable. The definition mistakes the tidiness of the set-theoretic resolutions for a feature of paradox-resolution in general.
passage:
The paradoxes that have been most successfully resolved in the history of logic share a common pattern. Russell's paradox arises from unrestricted comprehension, and is resolved by restricting set formation — the offending principle is clearly identified, clearly rejected, and the rejection dissolves the contradiction without residual difficulty. The Burali-Forti paradox arises from treating all ordinals as forming a set, and is resolved by denying that assumption. Cantor's paradox arises from an overly liberal notion of set, and is resolved by the set-class distinction. In each case, the resolution is clean: a single proposition is identified, rejected, and the difficulty evaporates.

One might therefore define paradox-resolution as requiring the identification of the paradox-generating propositions, the demonstration that exactly one is false or ill-formed, and the dissolution of the contradiction without new difficulties. This definition captures the structure of the successful set-theoretic resolutions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the sorites paradox — one of the oldest and most stubborn paradoxes in the philosophical tradition. The propositions are: a hundred thousand grains constitute a heap; if n grains constitute a heap then n minus one grains do as well; and a single grain does not constitute a heap. These are jointly inconsistent. The tolerance principle — proposition (b) — is the natural candidate for rejection. But rejecting it requires one to specify a sharp boundary: there must be some number n such that n grains constitute a heap and n minus one grains do not. This consequence conflicts with the manifest vagueness of the predicate 'heap' and strikes most philosophers as deeply implausible. The rejection of the offending proposition dissolves the original contradiction but generates a new difficulty of comparable philosophical magnitude. The definition's requirement that resolution proceed without new difficulties is not met, and the sorites remains unresolved in any fully satisfactory sense. The set-theoretic paradigm of clean resolution does not generalise to all paradoxes.

background_cases: The liar paradox — 'This sentence is false' — generates a contradiction because if the sentence is true then it is false, and if it is false then it is true; the paradox arises from the combination of self-reference and a truth predicate.

The Russell paradox — concerning the set of all sets that do not contain themselves — generates a contradiction because if the set contains itself then it does not, and if it does not then it does; the paradox arises from unrestricted set comprehension and self-membership.

The Grelling paradox — concerning the adjective 'heterological,' which is defined as applying to adjectives that do not apply to themselves — generates a contradiction because if 'heterological' is heterological then it is not, and if it is not then it is; the paradox arises from a self-referential predicate applied to linguistic items.
definition: A sentence or set of sentences generates a paradox if and only if (i) each sentence appears, individually, to be true or at least well-formed and meaningful, (ii) the sentences collectively entail a contradiction, and (iii) the contradiction arises from self-reference — that is, from a sentence or term that refers to or quantifies over itself.
conditions: Each sentence appears individually true or well-formed
They collectively entail a contradiction
The contradiction arises from self-reference
counterexample: Consider the Sorites paradox: a heap of sand consists of many grains; removing one grain from a heap leaves a heap; therefore, by iterated application, a single grain is a heap. Each premiss appears true — a heap is certainly many grains, and removing a single grain from a heap does not seem to destroy its heap-status. The premisses collectively entail the contradictory conclusion that a single grain is a heap. All the marks of paradox are present. Yet there is no self-reference in the Sorites — no sentence refers to itself, no term quantifies over itself. The contradiction arises from the vagueness of the predicate 'heap' and the repeated application of a tolerance principle, not from any reflexive structure. Condition (iii) is not satisfied.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the liar, Russell's paradox, the Grelling paradox — all involve self-reference or circularity as the mechanism that generates the contradiction. The definition has been constructed from these cases and identifies self-reference as the essential feature. But the Sorites paradox generates a contradiction through the iterative application of a vague predicate, with no self-reference whatsoever, revealing that the definition has mistaken a common feature of one family of paradoxes for a necessary condition of paradox as such.
passage:
The paradoxes that have attracted the most sustained attention in logic share a conspicuous structural feature. The liar paradox arises from a sentence that predicates falsity of itself. Russell's paradox arises from a set defined in terms of its own membership. The Grelling paradox arises from an adjective that is applied to itself. In each case, a reflexive loop — a sentence or term that bends back upon itself — is the mechanism that generates the contradiction.

One might codify these observations by defining a paradox as a set of individually plausible sentences that collectively entail a contradiction, where the contradiction arises from self-reference. This definition captures the structural commonality of the paradigmatic cases. The liar sentence refers to itself; the Russell set is defined in terms of self-membership; 'heterological' is applied to itself. The reflexive structure is the engine of contradiction. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the Sorites paradox. Begin with a heap of ten thousand grains of sand. The premiss that this is a heap is plainly true. The premiss that removing a single grain from a heap leaves a heap is highly plausible — the removal of one grain among ten thousand cannot transform a heap into a non-heap. But by iterating this premiss nine thousand nine hundred and ninety-nine times, one arrives at the conclusion that a single grain of sand is a heap, which is absurd. The premisses are individually plausible. They collectively entail a contradiction. But there is no self-reference in the argument: no sentence refers to itself, no predicate is applied to itself, no set is defined in terms of its own membership. The contradiction arises from the vagueness of the predicate 'heap' and the tolerance principle — the intuition that a small change in the number of grains cannot make the difference between heap and non-heap. The definition, built from the reflexive paradoxes, cannot accommodate this entirely different mechanism of paradox-generation.

background_cases: The inference from 'All observed ravens have been black' to 'All ravens are black' is an inductive inference that proceeds from a finite sample to a universal generalisation; its strength depends upon the number and variety of observations and the absence of any observed exception.

The inference from 'This drug has relieved headaches in every clinical trial' to 'This drug relieves headaches' proceeds from repeated controlled observations to a general causal claim; its strength depends upon the rigour of the trials and the consistency of the results.

The inference from 'Every spring for the past century, the river has flooded' to 'The river will flood next spring' proceeds from past regularity to a prediction about the future; its strength depends upon the stability of the conditions that produce the flooding.
definition: An inductive inference from premisses P to a conclusion C is strong if and only if (i) P reports a large and varied sample of observations, (ii) all observations in P support C — that is, no observation in P contradicts C, and (iii) C is a generalisation or prediction that extends beyond the observations reported in P.
conditions: P reports a large and varied sample
All observations support C
C extends beyond the reported observations
counterexample: Consider the inference: 'All pieces of gold examined so far have had an atomic number of 79; therefore all gold has an atomic number of 79.' The sample is large and varied — gold has been examined in every continent and every century. No observation contradicts the conclusion. The conclusion extends beyond the observed sample. All three conditions are met, and the inference is indeed strong. But now consider the inference: 'All pieces of gold examined so far have been found in the possession of a human being; therefore all gold is found in the possession of a human being.' The sample is equally large and varied, no observation contradicts the conclusion, and the conclusion extends beyond the sample. All three conditions are met with equal rigour. Yet this inference is plainly weak — gold exists in the earth's crust, on the ocean floor, and in asteroids, far from human possession. The definition cannot distinguish these two inferences.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve inferences in which the observed regularity is connected to an underlying mechanism or natural kind — the blackness of ravens is connected to their biology, the drug's efficacy to its pharmacology, the flooding to the river's hydrology. The strong inferences work because the regularity is grounded in the nature of the thing. The definition captures the observational and extensional features of these inferences but omits the requirement that the observed regularity be projectible — that it track a genuine natural property rather than an accidental or circumstantial correlation.
passage:
The inductive inferences most widely regarded as strong share a pattern that rewards careful analysis. The inference that all ravens are black rests upon a large and varied sample of observations, all of which support the generalisation. The inference that a drug relieves headaches rests upon consistent results across multiple controlled trials. The inference that the river will flood next spring rests upon a century of unbroken regularity. In each case, the observations are numerous, consistent, and the conclusion extends beyond what has been directly observed.

One might codify these observations by defining a strong inductive inference as one in which the premisses report a large and varied sample, all observations support the conclusion, and the conclusion extends beyond the sample. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the comparison of two superficially similar inferences. First: 'All examined pieces of gold have had an atomic number of 79; therefore all gold has atomic number 79.' Second: 'All examined pieces of gold have been found in the possession of a human being; therefore all gold is in human possession.' Both inferences satisfy the three conditions equally well — the samples are large and varied, no observation contradicts the conclusion, and the conclusion extends beyond the observed cases. Yet the first inference is compelling and the second is absurd. The difference lies not in the observational evidence but in the character of the property generalised. Atomic number is a property rooted in the nature of gold — it is what makes gold gold. Being in human possession is an accidental circumstance with no connection to the nature of the substance. The background cases all involved regularities grounded in the nature of the thing observed — biological, pharmacological, hydrological. The definition, by attending only to the size, variety, and consistency of the sample, cannot distinguish regularities that track genuine natural properties from regularities that are merely coincidental.

background_cases: The implication 'If it is raining, then the streets are wet' is regarded as a genuine implication because there is a causal connection between rain and wet streets — the rain produces the wetness, and the conditional captures this productive relation.

The implication 'If a figure is a square, then it has four sides' is regarded as genuine because the property of being a square conceptually includes the property of having four sides — the conditional captures a relation of conceptual containment.

The implication 'If the temperature drops below zero, then the water will freeze' is regarded as genuine because a well-understood physical process connects the antecedent to the consequent — the conditional expresses a nomological connection.
definition: A conditional 'If P then Q' expresses a genuine implication if and only if (i) the conditional is true — that is, it is not the case that P is true and Q false, (ii) there is a necessary connection between P and Q such that P could not be true without Q being true, and (iii) the connection between P and Q is knowable independently of knowing the truth-values of P and Q individually.
conditions: The conditional is true
There is a necessary connection between P and Q
The connection is knowable independently of the individual truth-values
counterexample: Consider the conditional 'If Goldbach's conjecture is true, then every even number greater than two is the sum of two primes.' This conditional is trivially true — the consequent merely restates the content of the antecedent. The connection between antecedent and consequent is as necessary as any logical relation could be. And the connection is knowable independently of knowing whether Goldbach's conjecture is true — one can see that the consequent restates the antecedent without knowing whether either is true. All three conditions are met. But now consider the conditional 'If this particular coin is tossed, it will land heads.' There is no necessary connection between tossing and landing heads — the outcome is contingent. Condition (ii) is violated, and the conditional does not express a genuine implication. Yet between these cases lies a vast and important class: statistical or probabilistic implications such as 'If a large sample is drawn from this population, approximately 50% will be male.' The connection is not necessary — any particular sample might deviate — yet the implication is genuine and scientifically indispensable. The definition excludes it.
abductive_insight: The background cases involve connections that are either causal (rain and wet streets, temperature and freezing) or conceptual (squares and four sides), and in each case the connection is necessary within its domain. The definition captures this necessity requirement. But scientific practice relies heavily upon statistical and probabilistic implications — connections that hold with high probability rather than with necessity — and the definition's requirement of necessary connection excludes this important class, revealing that genuine implication extends beyond necessity to encompass well-grounded probabilistic relations.
passage:
The conditionals most readily accepted as expressing genuine implications share a feature that invites generalisation. The conditional connecting rain to wet streets rests upon a causal mechanism: the rain produces the wetness. The conditional connecting squareness to four-sidedness rests upon a conceptual relation: the concept of a square includes the concept of four sides. The conditional connecting sub-zero temperature to freezing rests upon a well-understood physical process: the reduction of molecular motion below the threshold required to maintain the liquid state. In each case, the connection between antecedent and consequent is necessary within its domain — causally, conceptually, or nomologically.

One might codify these observations by defining a genuine implication as a true conditional in which there is a necessary connection between antecedent and consequent, knowable independently of the individual truth-values. This definition captures the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises from the scientific practice of probabilistic reasoning. Consider the conditional 'If a fair coin is tossed one thousand times, approximately half the outcomes will be heads.' This conditional expresses a genuine and scientifically important implication — it follows from well-confirmed probability theory and is the basis of statistical inference. Yet the connection is not necessary in the sense the definition requires: any particular sequence of one thousand tosses might, by chance, produce ninety percent heads. The probability of this is vanishingly small, but it is not zero, and the conditional does not hold with the necessity that the definition demands. The background cases all involved connections that were necessary within their domains. The definition captures this feature faithfully but in doing so excludes the large and indispensable class of probabilistic implications, in which the connection between antecedent and consequent is well-grounded and scientifically rigorous but holds with high probability rather than with strict necessity.

background_cases: The analogy between the atom and the solar system — electrons orbiting a nucleus as planets orbit a star — was fruitful because the structural similarity between the two systems (a central body with orbiting smaller bodies) allowed physicists to apply well-understood gravitational mathematics to the new domain of atomic structure.

The analogy between electrical circuits and hydraulic systems — current as water flow, voltage as water pressure, resistance as pipe narrowness — was fruitful because the structural similarity allowed engineers to use their intuitive understanding of fluid dynamics to reason about the less intuitive behaviour of electrical current.

The analogy between natural selection and artificial selection — nature 'selecting' fit organisms as a breeder selects desirable livestock — was fruitful because the well-understood mechanism of artificial selection made the unfamiliar concept of natural selection intelligible by mapping its structure onto a familiar practice.
definition: An argument by analogy from a source domain S to a target domain T is strong if and only if (i) S and T share a significant number of relevant properties, (ii) the property inferred to hold of T on the basis of the analogy is known to hold of S, and (iii) there are no known relevant dissimilarities between S and T that would undermine the inference.
conditions: S and T share significant relevant properties
The inferred property holds of S
No known relevant dissimilarities undermine the inference
counterexample: Consider the analogy between the human brain and a digital computer: both process information, both have memory and storage, both take inputs and produce outputs, both can perform calculations. These shared properties are numerous and seem relevant. Computability — the capacity to execute any algorithm — is known to hold of digital computers. No obvious dissimilarity appears to undermine the inference. All three conditions are met. Yet the inference that the brain is therefore a computational device in the same sense as a digital computer has been powerfully contested: the brain operates through massively parallel electrochemical processes, not sequential symbol manipulation; its 'memory' is distributed and content-addressable, not stored at discrete addresses; its outputs are shaped by embodiment and affect in ways that have no computational analogue. The relevant dissimilarities exist but are not obvious until one looks beneath the surface properties.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the atom and the solar system, circuits and hydraulics, natural and artificial selection — all involve analogies in which the shared properties reflect a shared underlying structure or mechanism. The structural similarity is not merely a surface resemblance but a deep correspondence in the way the systems operate. The definition captures the surface features — shared properties, absence of disanalogies — but does not require that the shared properties reflect a genuine structural correspondence, and thereby cannot distinguish analogies that track real structural similarities from analogies that rest upon superficial resemblances that dissolve under closer examination.
passage:
The analogies most celebrated in the history of science and philosophy share a feature that is easily overlooked. The analogy between the atom and the solar system was fruitful not merely because both systems involve small bodies orbiting a larger one — a surface resemblance that might be accidental — but because the mathematical structure governing orbital dynamics could be transferred from one domain to the other, yielding quantitative predictions. The analogy between circuits and hydraulics was fruitful because the equations governing fluid flow in pipes are structurally identical to those governing current in wires. The analogy between natural and artificial selection was fruitful because the mechanism of differential reproduction operates in both domains. In each case, the shared properties reflect a shared underlying structure.

One might codify these observations by defining a strong analogy as one in which the source and target share significant relevant properties, the inferred property holds of the source, and there are no known relevant dissimilarities. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the analogy between the brain and a digital computer. The shared properties are numerous and appear relevant: both process information, both have memory, both take inputs and produce outputs, both perform calculations. The capacity for computation is known to hold of digital computers. No obvious dissimilarity presents itself to the casual observer. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the inference that the brain is a computational device in the same sense as a digital computer has been powerfully challenged. The brain's operations are massively parallel and electrochemical, not sequential and symbolic. Its memory is distributed and associative, not discretely addressed. Its processing is shaped by embodiment, affect, and context in ways that have no computational analogue. These dissimilarities are real and deep, but they are not apparent until one moves beyond the surface properties to the underlying mechanisms. The definition attends to shared properties and the absence of known disanalogies but does not require that the shared properties reflect a genuine structural correspondence — and it is this deeper requirement that distinguishes the fruitful analogies from the misleading ones.

background_cases: The argument 'All mammals are warm-blooded; all whales are mammals; therefore all whales are warm-blooded' is recognised as valid because its conclusion follows from its premisses by the rule of the categorical syllogism, and this can be verified by constructing the appropriate syllogistic figure.

The argument 'If it rains then the ground is wet; it rains; therefore the ground is wet' is recognised as valid because its conclusion follows from its premisses by the rule of modus ponens, and this can be verified by a simple truth-table.

The argument 'Either the butler or the gardener committed the crime; the butler did not commit the crime; therefore the gardener committed the crime' is recognised as valid because its conclusion follows from its premisses by the rule of disjunctive syllogism, and this too can be verified truth-functionally.
definition: An argument is valid if and only if (i) it consists of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) its conclusion can be derived from its premisses by the application of recognised rules of inference, and (iii) each rule of inference employed is truth-preserving — that is, it never leads from true premisses to a false conclusion.
conditions: The argument consists of premisses and a conclusion
The conclusion can be derived from the premisses by recognised rules of inference
Each rule employed is truth-preserving
counterexample: Consider the following argument in the predicate calculus: 'There is something that is loved by everyone; therefore, everyone loves something.' This argument is valid — its conclusion must be true if its premiss is true. Yet it is not an instance of any single recognised rule of inference of the sort cited in condition (ii). It requires a sequence of moves involving quantifier manipulation — instantiation, re-ordering, and re-generalisation — that collectively preserve truth but do not correspond to any single named rule. One might formalise the derivation, but the validity of the argument is grasped prior to and independently of any particular formalisation. The argument is valid in virtue of the logical relations among its quantificational structures, not in virtue of conformity to a pre-existing catalogue of rules.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve arguments whose validity is captured by a single, familiar rule of inference — the syllogism, modus ponens, disjunctive syllogism. The definition codifies this pattern by requiring derivability via recognised rules. Yet the quantificational argument reveals that validity may depend on the logical structure of the premisses and conclusion in ways that are not reducible to the application of any single named rule. The definition mistakes the means by which we demonstrate validity — the application of rules — for the ground of validity itself, which resides in the logical relations among the propositions.
passage:
The arguments most straightforwardly recognised as valid tend to be those whose validity is captured by a single, familiar rule of inference. The categorical syllogism licenses the passage from 'All mammals are warm-blooded' and 'All whales are mammals' to 'All whales are warm-blooded.' Modus ponens licenses the passage from a conditional and its antecedent to its consequent. Disjunctive syllogism licenses the passage from a disjunction and the negation of one disjunct to the other. In each case, the validity is transparent: one can see at a glance that the rule preserves truth.

One might attempt to codify this transparency by the following definition: an argument is valid if and only if it consists of premisses and a conclusion, the conclusion can be derived from the premisses by recognised rules of inference, and each rule is truth-preserving. This definition captures the character of the background cases with admirable clarity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the logic of quantification. Consider the argument: there is something that is loved by everyone; therefore, everyone loves something. This argument is valid — if the premiss is true, the conclusion must be true. Yet its validity does not consist in the application of any single recognised rule of inference. It requires a sequence of quantifier manipulations — universal and existential instantiation, re-ordering of variables, and re-generalisation — that collectively preserve truth but do not correspond to any named rule in the standard catalogue. The validity of the argument is grasped through an understanding of the logical relations among its quantificational structures, prior to and independently of any particular formalisation. One might observe that the definition, by requiring derivability via recognised rules, mistakes the instrument by which we demonstrate validity for the ground of validity itself. The background cases are perspicuous because their validity happens to coincide with a single rule; the quantificational case reveals that validity may outrun any pre-existing rule catalogue.

background_cases: The proposition 'John is a bachelor' entails the proposition 'John is unmarried' — the entailment holds in virtue of the fact that the meaning of 'bachelor' includes the condition of being unmarried, and one cannot coherently affirm the first proposition whilst denying the second.

The proposition 'Today is Tuesday' entails the proposition 'Today is a weekday' — the entailment holds because the meaning of 'Tuesday' is such that it falls within the class of weekdays, and the first proposition cannot be true without the second being true.

The proposition 'This figure is a square' entails the proposition 'This figure is a rectangle' — the entailment holds because the definition of a square includes being a rectangle with equal sides, and one cannot coherently assert that a figure is a square whilst denying it is a rectangle.
definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) P cannot be true whilst Q is false, (ii) the impossibility of P's being true and Q's being false holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms in P and Q, and (iii) the entailment can be recognised by any competent speaker who understands the meanings of the terms involved.
conditions: P cannot be true whilst Q is false
The impossibility holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms
The entailment can be recognised by any competent speaker who understands the terms
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'Water is present in this vessel' and the proposition 'H2O is present in this vessel.' It is impossible for the first to be true and the second false, since water is H2O. Yet a competent speaker of English who has never studied chemistry might perfectly well understand the ordinary meaning of 'water' without recognising that its presence entails the presence of H2O. Condition (iii) fails. Moreover, it is debatable whether the impossibility holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms rather than in virtue of a substantive empirical discovery about the composition of water. Condition (ii) is at best controversial.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve entailments that can be grasped through an understanding of ordinary linguistic meaning — 'bachelor' means unmarried man, 'Tuesday' is a weekday, 'square' is an equilateral rectangle. The definition codifies this semantic transparency as a necessary condition. Yet the water-H2O case reveals that some entailments hold necessarily but are not graspable through meaning alone: they depend upon substantive discoveries about the nature of the things the terms designate. The definition mistakes a conspicuous class of entailments — those grounded in semantic conventions — for the full extent of the entailment relation.
passage:
The entailments most readily recognised tend to be those that can be grasped by anyone who understands the meanings of the terms involved. That a bachelor is unmarried, that Tuesday is a weekday, that a square is a rectangle — these entailments are transparent to any competent speaker. They hold in virtue of the meanings of the terms, and their recognition requires no specialised information beyond an ordinary command of the language.

One might codify this pattern by the following definition: a proposition entails another if and only if the first cannot be true whilst the second is false, this impossibility holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms, and the entailment can be recognised by any competent speaker who understands those meanings. This definition captures the semantic ground of the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the entailment between 'water is present' and 'H2O is present.' It is impossible for the first to be true and the second false, since water is H2O — this is a fact about the nature of the substance, established by chemical investigation. Yet a competent English speaker who has never studied chemistry might understand the ordinary meaning of 'water' perfectly well without recognising that its presence entails the presence of H2O. The entailment is not transparent to ordinary linguistic understanding. Moreover, it is debatable whether the impossibility holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms or in virtue of a substantive empirical discovery. One might observe that the background cases are characterised by what one might call semantic entailment — entailment grounded in the conventions of language — whilst the water case involves what might be called natural entailment, grounded in the nature of the substance itself. The definition, by requiring semantic transparency, cannot accommodate this second, equally genuine species of entailment.

background_cases: The conditional 'If this wire is copper, then it conducts electricity' is accepted as true because there is a well-understood causal connection between being made of copper and conducting electricity; the antecedent and consequent are linked by a physical law, and the conditional expresses this lawful connection.

The conditional 'If the barometer falls, then a storm is approaching' is accepted as true because the fall of the barometer is a reliable indicator of an approaching storm; there is a recognised empirical connection between the antecedent and the consequent, mediated by atmospheric pressure.

The conditional 'If a number is divisible by four, then it is divisible by two' is accepted as true because the mathematical relationship between divisibility by four and divisibility by two is one of logical entailment; the antecedent guarantees the consequent in virtue of the structure of the integers.
definition: A conditional 'if P then Q' is true if and only if (i) it is not the case that P is true and Q is false, (ii) there exists a connection — causal, logical, or evidential — between the state of affairs described by P and that described by Q, and (iii) the connection is such that the truth of P provides a reason for expecting the truth of Q.
conditions: It is not the case that P is true and Q is false
There exists a causal, logical, or evidential connection between P and Q
The truth of P provides a reason for expecting the truth of Q
counterexample: Consider the conditional 'If Paris is in Germany, then two plus two equals four.' The antecedent is false and the consequent is true, so condition (i) is satisfied — it is not the case that the antecedent is true and the consequent false. Yet there is no connection whatever — causal, logical, or evidential — between the geographical location of Paris and the truths of arithmetic. Conditions (ii) and (iii) fail. In classical logic, however, this conditional is true: a material conditional with a false antecedent is true regardless of the consequent. The definition excludes a class of conditionals that the standard logic of propositions accepts as true.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve conditionals in which a substantive connection links the antecedent and the consequent — a causal law, an empirical regularity, a mathematical entailment. The definition extracts this connection as a necessary condition. Yet the case of the conditional with a false antecedent and an unrelated true consequent reveals a tension between the definition and the material conditional of classical logic. The background cases are the conditionals we ordinarily assert and rely upon, but classical logic admits a broader class of true conditionals that lack any connection between antecedent and consequent. The definition captures the ordinary notion of a conditional but not the logical one, suggesting that these are two distinct concepts that any adequate account must disentangle.
passage:
The conditionals we most readily accept as true tend to be those in which a substantive connection links the antecedent to the consequent. The conditional about copper and conductivity is underwritten by a physical law. The conditional about the barometer and the storm rests upon a well-established empirical regularity. The conditional about divisibility by four and divisibility by two holds in virtue of a mathematical entailment. In each case, the truth of the antecedent provides a genuine reason for expecting the truth of the consequent, and the connection between them is intelligible.

One might attempt to codify this pattern by the following definition: a conditional 'if P then Q' is true if and only if it is not the case that the antecedent is true and the consequent false, there exists a causal, logical, or evidential connection between the states of affairs described, and the truth of the antecedent provides a reason for expecting the truth of the consequent. This definition captures the connected character of the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency — or at least its tension with the standard logical treatment — arises with conditionals whose antecedents are false and whose consequents are unrelated truths. Consider the conditional 'If Paris is in Germany, then two plus two equals four.' The antecedent is false and the consequent is true, so condition (i) is satisfied. Yet there is no connection whatever between the geographical location of Paris and the truths of arithmetic. Conditions (ii) and (iii) fail entirely. In classical propositional logic, however, this conditional is true: the material conditional is true whenever the antecedent is false. One might observe that the definition captures our ordinary practice of conditional reasoning, in which we demand a connection between antecedent and consequent, but it does not capture the material conditional of formal logic, which admits true conditionals without any such connection. The background cases, it appears, instantiate a richer notion of conditionality than the truth-functional analysis provides.

background_cases: The Liar paradox — 'This sentence is false' — generates a contradiction because the sentence refers to itself: if it is true, then what it says is the case, so it is false; and if it is false, then it is not the case that it is false, so it is true. The paradox arises from self-reference.

Russell's paradox concerns the set of all sets that do not contain themselves: if this set contains itself, then by its defining condition it does not; and if it does not contain itself, then by its defining condition it does. The paradox arises from the self-referential definition of the set.

Grelling's paradox asks whether the word 'heterological' — defined as a word that does not describe itself — is itself heterological: if it is, then it describes itself and so is not; if it is not, then it fails to describe itself and so is. The paradox turns upon the word's self-referential application.
definition: A paradox arises if and only if (i) an apparently sound piece of reasoning leads to a contradiction or an absurd conclusion, (ii) the reasoning proceeds from premisses that seem individually unexceptionable, and (iii) the contradiction arises from the self-referential character of one of the terms or propositions involved.
conditions: An apparently sound piece of reasoning leads to a contradiction or absurdity
The reasoning proceeds from individually unexceptionable premisses
The contradiction arises from the self-referential character of a term or proposition
counterexample: Consider Zeno's paradox of the arrow. At every instant of its flight, the arrow occupies a position exactly equal to its own length. At no single instant is the arrow in motion — motion requires the occupation of different positions, and at an instant there is no duration in which to change position. Yet the arrow plainly moves. The reasoning proceeds from unexceptionable premisses — that at each instant the arrow is somewhere, and that at an instant there is no change — and leads to the absurd conclusion that motion is impossible. Yet no self-reference is involved. The paradox arises from the analysis of continuity and the relation between instants and intervals, not from any term or proposition that refers to itself.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Liar, Russell's set, Grelling's paradox — all arise from self-reference, and the definition codifies self-reference as a necessary condition of paradox. Yet Zeno's arrow reveals that paradox may arise from deep conceptual difficulties — in this case, the nature of continuity, instantaneous states, and motion — that have nothing to do with self-reference. The definition captures one important family of paradoxes but mistakes it for the whole, failing to accommodate paradoxes whose source lies in the tension between apparently sound principles about non-self-referential matters.
passage:
The paradoxes most frequently discussed in the logical literature share a common structural feature: self-reference. The Liar sentence refers to itself. Russell's set is defined by a condition that applies to itself. Grelling's word is tested against itself. In each case, the contradiction is generated by the reflexive application of a concept or expression to its own case.

One might codify this observation by the following definition: a paradox arises when apparently sound reasoning from individually unexceptionable premisses leads to a contradiction, and the contradiction arises from the self-referential character of one of the terms or propositions involved. This definition captures the mechanism of the background paradoxes with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in a quite different quarter. Zeno's paradox of the arrow invites us to consider an arrow in flight. At every instant of its flight, the arrow occupies a definite position exactly equal to its own length. At no single instant is the arrow in motion, for motion requires the occupation of different positions, and at an instant — a durationless point of time — there is no opportunity for change of position. Yet the arrow plainly moves from one place to another. The premisses are individually unexceptionable, the reasoning appears sound, and the conclusion — that the arrow does not move — is absurd. Yet no self-reference is involved anywhere in the argument. The paradox arises from a tension between our concept of an instant and our concept of motion, from the difficulty of reconciling the continuous character of motion with its analysis into durationless points. One might observe that the definition, by restricting paradox to cases of self-reference, fails to accommodate the ancient and enduring paradoxes of continuity and change.

background_cases: The syllogism 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal' is valid because its conclusion follows from its premises by virtue of the logical form alone — the form 'All A are B; x is A; therefore x is B' — regardless of the specific content substituted for A, B, and x.

The argument 'If it is raining then the streets are wet; it is raining; therefore the streets are wet' is valid because its conclusion follows by modus ponens, a rule that preserves truth in virtue of the logical structure of the conditional, irrespective of the particular propositions involved.

The argument 'No reptiles are mammals; all snakes are reptiles; therefore no snakes are mammals' is valid because its conclusion follows from the form 'No A are B; all C are A; therefore no C are B' — a form that guarantees truth-preservation under every substitution.
definition: An argument is deductively valid if and only if (i) its conclusion follows from its premises in virtue of its logical form, (ii) no substitution of non-logical vocabulary into the same form produces true premises and a false conclusion, and (iii) the logical form of the argument can be expressed within a standard formal language.
conditions: Its conclusion follows from its premises in virtue of its logical form
No substitution of non-logical vocabulary into the same form produces true premises and a false conclusion
The logical form of the argument can be expressed within a standard formal language
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'This object is red; therefore this object is coloured.' The conclusion follows necessarily from the premise — nothing can be red without being coloured. No possible circumstance makes the premise true and the conclusion false. Yet the necessity does not rest upon logical form: the form 'x is F; therefore x is G' is not in general valid; it is valid here only because of the specific semantic relationship between 'red' and 'coloured.' No standard formal language captures this relationship as a matter of form. Conditions (i) and (iii) are not satisfied, yet the argument is necessarily truth-preserving.
abductive_insight: The background cases all exhibit a common feature: the truth-preservation of each argument is entirely independent of the meanings of its non-logical terms. background_cases: In classical propositional logic, the set {P, P implies Q} has Q as a logical consequence: in every truth-value assignment that makes both P and P-implies-Q true, Q is also true. The consequence relation holds because of the truth-functional meaning of the conditional.

The consequence relation holds because of the semantic rules governing the universal quantifier and predication.

In modal logic S5, the set {Necessarily P} has P as a logical consequence: in every model in which necessarily-P holds at a world, P holds at that world. The consequence relation holds because of the accessibility relation governing the necessity operator.

But by the undecidability of first-order arithmetic, there is no algorithm that will determine in general whether a given sentence is a consequence of the Peano axioms. The definition conflates the metaphysics of consequence with the conditions of its recognition.
